entries.prompt: Walk me through a moment from the last few days that's still rattling around in your head — what happened, who was there, and what you keep coming back to about it.
entries.content:
The thing I keep coming back to happened Tuesday at the grocery store, of all places, which is annoying because it would be more dignified if it had happened somewhere with stakes. I was in the cereal aisle and I ran into Marcus, who I haven't seen in maybe two years, from two jobs ago. He was in a quarter-zip and looked like he had just come from somewhere with a parking validation. He asked what I was up to, the question, and I gave the answer I have been rehearsing in some form since January — something about coaching, something about consulting, something gestural about "figuring out the right shape." And he did the thing where his face moved before his mouth did. Just a flicker. Then he said "good for you, that sounds like a real reset," and we talked about his kid's hockey for a minute and he moved on toward dairy.

I stood there with a box of the cereal Nina likes in my hand and I could not move for what felt like a long time but was probably eight seconds. Real reset. The way someone says "good for you" to a person who just announced they are going to write a novel. The encouragement that is also a small obituary.

What gets me is that I don't even think Marcus meant anything by it. He's a decent person. He was being kind in the only register he had available, which is the register of people who are still on the thing. From inside the thing, what I'm doing has to be either a sabbatical or a breakdown or a brave reinvention, because there isn't a vocabulary for "I left and I don't fully know yet." That sentence makes everyone uncomfortable, including me when I have to say it out loud to a man holding two boxes of granola.

I came home and Jamie was at the kitchen table with the laptop open, and I almost told her about it and didn't. Not because I was hiding it, but because I could already hear how it would land — Jamie would say something supportive and reasonable, and I would feel a little better, and I would also feel like I had used up some of her supportiveness on a thing that probably doesn't deserve it. She has a finite amount of "tell me about your day, sweetheart" energy and she's also coordinating Theo's orthodontist and figuring out who's driving Nina to the Friday thing. So I just said hi and put the groceries away and the dog watched me from the rug like she does.

What I keep turning over is not the encounter itself. It's that for about an hour afterward I drafted, in my head, a version of my life I could have told Marcus that would have made his face do a different thing. I had a whole imaginary LinkedIn post written by the time I was unloading the car. "Excited to share that I am" — and then a noun, and the noun is the problem. I don't have the noun yet. I might not have the noun for another six months. I might not get the noun. And the part of me that wants the noun is not, I think, the honest part. The honest part is the one that has been getting up at 6:15 and reading for forty minutes before anyone else is awake, and that part is fine, that part is actually good, that part doesn't need a quarter-zip approval at all. But the noun-wanting part is loud and it knows my passwords.

There's a thing my dad used to say about how you can tell what a man does for a living by how he answers the question at a barbecue. He said it admiringly. He meant: a real person has a clean answer. I have been carrying that around for forty-seven years apparently, because here I am in a cereal aisle feeling judged by a guy I haven't thought about in two years for not having a clean answer. My dad has been gone six years and he is still in the cereal aisle with me. That's something.

The other thing — and this is the one I actually want to write down because I haven't said it anywhere — is that on the drive home I had this small thought that surprised me. It was something like: Marcus is going back to the office, and I am going home to my kitchen, and I would not trade. Even on the bad days, even with the spreadsheets at 11 p.m., even with Mom calling to ask if I've "had any news," I would not trade. That was the thought. And then immediately I distrusted it, because it sounded like the kind of thing a person tells themselves to feel better, and I have spent a long career being suspicious of feelings that arrive conveniently. But it didn't feel convenient. It felt like a fact I noticed sideways, the way you notice a bird when you're looking at something else.

I haven't told Jamie about the Marcus thing. I might. I might not. Nina came home and asked me what's for dinner and I said pasta and she said "again?" with the precise emphasis of someone who has just discovered rhetoric, and then Theo came down and ate a string cheese standing up at the counter without saying anything, which is his entire personality right now, and the moment got buried under the regular avalanche of evening, the way moments do.

But it's still here Thursday morning. So apparently it isn't buried. Apparently I'm going to be in that cereal aisle for a while.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a time recently when you said something out loud that you'd been editing in your head for a while — what came out, who heard it, and how it landed compared to how you'd rehearsed it.
entries.content:
Yesterday afternoon I told Pat that I wasn't sure the thing we've been talking about should be a thing, and the words came out cleaner than I expected, and then sat in the air between us in a way I had not rehearsed for at all.

We were at the coffee place near the library, the one with the long communal table where you have to pretend the person across from you isn't there. Pat had brought a printout — Pat still prints things, which is one of the reasons I like Pat — of a rough plan for a kind of small joint offering, the language for which we have been workshopping over text for about three weeks. Two-day workshop, small cohort, a name we both half-liked. I had spent Tuesday night writing out what I wanted to say. Not a script, exactly, but the shape of it. I had a version where I led with appreciation. I had a version where I led with a question. I had a version, my favorite for about an hour, where I gestured at "timing" and made it sound like a logistics problem and not a me problem.

What actually came out, after Pat slid the printout across and said "okay, gut check," was: "I think I might be using this to avoid figuring out what I actually want to do."

I heard it the way you hear your own voice on a recording. Slightly higher than I expected. Pat's face did a thing that I have been thinking about ever since — not a flicker like Marcus's, more like a small adjustment, the way someone reseats themselves in a chair. Pat said "okay" and then "say more about that," which is the Pat move, and I did, sort of. I said something about how every time we talk about the joint thing I feel a kind of relief, and that the relief is suspicious to me, because it feels like the relief of having something to point at when Mom calls, more than the relief of having figured out the work. I said the partnership made the answer to "what are you up to" easier, and that was maybe doing too much of the work. I said I wasn't pulling out, I was just — and then I lost the sentence and drank coffee.

What I had rehearsed was more elegant. In the rehearsed version I was going to say something about wanting to honor the conversation by being honest about my own clarity, or the lack of it, and Pat was going to say something gracious, and we were going to leave the printout on the table as a "maybe later" and walk out feeling like we had done something mature. In the actual version I just sat there having said a slightly clumsier and more honest thing, and Pat sat there receiving it, and there was a long pause where the espresso machine made its noises and someone behind us was on a phone call about a flight.

Then Pat said, "I think I've been doing the same thing." Which I did not have a rehearsed response to.

We talked for another forty minutes. Pat said something about how it's easier to build a thing with someone than to sit with the question of whether you should be building anything at all. I said something about how the two of us are good at making each other feel like we're being productive, and how that might not be the same as being productive. We did not put the printout away. It stayed on the table the whole time, with little rings of coffee on the corner where Pat had set the cup down. By the end we had not exactly decided anything. We had agreed to keep talking but not to do anything for at least a month. I felt lighter and also slightly bereft, because the joint thing had been giving me something to do with my hands, metaphorically, and now I had to put my hands down again.

What's strange is that I think the part I had been most afraid of saying — that I might be using Pat — Pat heard not as an insult but as something close to a relief. I had built up the sentence in my head for so many days that I had given it a weight it didn't actually have when it landed. The rehearsed version was always going to be worse than the actual version, because the rehearsed version was for a Pat I had constructed who would be hurt, and the real Pat just... wasn't. The real Pat had been carrying a similar sentence around in a different pocket.

I drove home and the light was that late-October light where everything looks more serious than it is, and I thought about how much energy I had spent editing that sentence and how the editing had been, mostly, a way of not saying it. The longer I rehearse a thing the more I am, I think, trying to make it not need to be said. Like if I phrase it well enough it will dissolve in transit. And then it doesn't dissolve, it just gets said, and the reception is almost never what I built.

I told Jamie about it at dinner, briefly, while Nina was telling Theo about something a teacher did. Jamie said "huh, good," and reached for the salad, and that was the right amount. I had been worried she would want to talk about it more, or less, and instead she just absorbed it and passed the bowl. The dog was under the table waiting for something to fall.

The thing I keep coming back to is not what I said to Pat. It's that I said it at all, and that the version that came out was less polished than the one I had prepared, and that the less polished one worked better. Which is a lesson I have apparently been refusing to learn for several decades. I don't know what I'll do with the month. I don't know if Pat and I will pick the printout back up or quietly let it become a thing we don't mention. But the printout is on my kitchen table now, under a school form and a vet reminder, and every time I see the corner of it I feel something I don't have a name for, which is becoming a theme.

entries.prompt: Think about a recent stretch of time where you weren't doing anything in particular — driving somewhere, washing dishes, lying awake before sleep. What did your mind drift to without you steering it, and what did you find yourself saying back to it?
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon I was raking leaves in the side yard, the part by the fence where the maple drops everything in about a four-day window and then is done. Jamie was inside on a call, the kids were in their respective screens, the dog was doing her job which is to lie on the pile and then look offended when I move it. It was that kind of raking where after a while your arms know what to do and your head goes somewhere else, and what I found was that my head went, without my permission, to a meeting from probably 2014.

I don't know why this meeting. There was nothing special about it. It was the kind of mid-afternoon room where someone had brought cookies that nobody wanted, and I was presenting something — I can't even remember what — and a senior person whose name I would rather not write down nodded at me in a particular way at a particular moment and I felt, for about ninety seconds, like the most competent person in the building. That was the whole memory. Just the nod and the ninety seconds. And there I was, eleven years later, holding a rake, replaying it like it was a song I used to like.

What I said back to it, after a minute, was something like: oh. Oh, that's what you're doing. You're shopping for evidence.

Because that's what it was. My head had gone looking, unprompted, for a moment when I had been seen as good at something, and it had pulled this one up from deep storage and held it out to me like a kid showing you a rock. *Look. You used to be like this. Remember?* And the part of me that received the rock was grateful and also a little embarrassed, the way you are when someone sends you a flattering photo of yourself from a long time ago and you save it but you don't tell anyone you saved it.

I kept raking. The pile got bigger. The dog moved. And the thing I noticed — and I am writing this down because I want to be honest about it — is that the memory wasn't actually about the work. I couldn't tell you what the work was. The memory was about being approved of by someone whose approval I had decided counted. And what I have not had, in nine months, is anyone whose approval counts in that specific architectural way. Jamie's approval counts but it's domestic, it's the approval of a person who has seen me in a robe. Pat's approval counts but Pat is a peer, which is different. Mom's approval — well. The whole point of this stretch is supposed to be that I'm not arranging my life around the nods of people in conference rooms anymore. And I have been telling myself, mostly successfully, that I don't miss it. And then on a Sunday in October my own brain, unsupervised, goes and digs up a nod from 2014 and presents it like a relic.

What I said back to it, after another minute of raking, was: that's fine. You can miss it. You can miss it and also not want it back. Those can both be true. You spent twenty-some years in rooms where being good at the thing was the same as being a person, and now you're in a yard, and the conversion rate is bad. Of course your head is doing this. It's the same head.

I don't know if that counts as a kind answer or a managerial one. It felt true when I said it.

The other thing my mind did, while I was bagging the leaves into those tall paper bags that always tear at the bottom, was a small unsolicited inventory of people from that life and where they are now. Not Marcus, who I already accidentally inventoried in the cereal aisle. But others. The one who got promoted twice in eighteen months. The one who left and started something that's apparently doing well, based on a thing Chris mentioned in a text I didn't fully respond to. The one who I always thought was smarter than me and is now doing something I would have called boring at thirty and now think might be wise. I went through them one by one without meaning to, like flipping through a deck of cards. And I noticed — this is the part I want to remember — I noticed that I wasn't measuring myself against them in the comparison sense. I was checking on them. The way you check on people you used to share a kitchen with. Just: are you okay over there. Just that.

Which surprised me. Because the version of this drift I would have predicted, if you had asked me in January, was the comparison version. The LinkedIn-scroll version. And what showed up instead was something gentler and more like — I don't know — alumni feeling. Like I had graduated from something without meaning to, and these were my classmates, and I hoped they were eating well.

I tied off the last bag and stood there for a second with my hands on my lower back like a person twice my age, and I thought: the noun-wanting part wasn't loud today. That was the surprise. The noun-wanting part was quiet, and what spoke up instead was this older softer thing that just wanted to know everyone was okay, including me. Including, I noticed, the version of me from 2014 in that meeting room, who was pleased with himself for ninety seconds and didn't know what was coming, and who I am apparently, finally, willing to be a little tender toward instead of using as a yardstick.

Then Nina opened the back door and yelled something about a hoodie and the spell broke and I went inside.

entries.prompt: When you describe what you're up to these days, what's the version you wish you could give — not the polished one, not the gestural one, but the one that would actually feel like the truth?
entries.content:
The version I wish I could give would start with the kettle. I don't know why, but if someone asked me on a Saturday morning what I'm up to and I were allowed to answer truthfully and at any length, I think I would start by describing the kettle, and the fact that I am awake before everyone else, and that the dog has learned to come downstairs with me now because I am a more reliable source of early-morning movement than I used to be. I would say that for the first time in twenty-some years I know what my own house sounds like at 6:30 in the morning, and that this turns out to be information I had been missing without knowing I was missing it.

That is not, of course, what anyone is asking. What they are asking is: what are you doing for money, or what will you be doing for money, or have you figured out the noun yet. And the truthful version has to acknowledge that question because pretending it isn't there would be its own kind of dishonesty. So the truthful version, if I had the room for it, would go something like:

I left a thing I was good at because being good at it had stopped being the same as wanting to do it, and I did not have a next thing lined up, which I know is the part everyone wants to fix for me. I have some money. Not enough to do this forever, but enough to do it for a while longer if we don't get extravagant, and we are not extravagant people. I am exploring — no, not that word — I am poking at some adjacent work, coaching-ish, consulting-ish, the kind of work that the old me would have called soft and that I now suspect is harder than it looks. I have a peer I am sometimes working with and sometimes not, and we recently agreed not to make any moves for a month, which is the most honest decision I have made all year. I read a lot. I take walks. I am present for my kids in a way I was not when they were smaller, and this is both a gift to them and, I am beginning to suspect, a small grief, because it makes me notice what I missed.

I do not know yet what this becomes. I have a working hypothesis that the answer will not arrive as a noun but as something more like a direction, the way you can't always name the city you're driving toward but you know it's east. I am uncomfortable saying that out loud because it sounds either spiritual or evasive, and I am neither of those things by temperament. I am a person who used to make slide decks. I would prefer the noun. I am learning, slowly and with a lot of resistance, that the wanting of the noun is part of what I left, and that if I produce a noun too quickly to satisfy the wanting, I will have just rebuilt the thing I left in a smaller room.

That would be the truthful version. And I notice, writing it out, that nobody has the bandwidth for it. Not Marcus in the cereal aisle, not Mom on the phone, not even Jamie really, not because Jamie wouldn't listen but because Jamie is living it alongside me and doesn't need the speech. The truthful version is too long for any actual conversation. Which is maybe why the gestural one keeps winning — not because I prefer it, but because it fits in the time allotted.

What I keep getting wrong, I think, is assuming that the polished version and the truthful version are different in content. They aren't. They're different in length. The polished version is the truthful version with most of the uncertainty edited out, and the gestural version is the polished version with most of the specifics edited out, and what's left at the end of all that editing is a sentence so smooth it barely says anything. "Figuring out the right shape." That sentence is a stone someone has been turning over in a river for a thousand years.

If I really had to compress it, if Marcus was going to be in the cereal aisle for forty seconds and not forty minutes, the version I would actually want to give is something like: I left because I needed to, and I'm taking the time to find out what the next thing is, and I don't know yet, and I'm okay. Six clauses. The last one is the one I have the hardest time saying because I can't always vouch for it. Some days I'm okay and some days I am refreshing a blank document for an hour and texting Pat about something that doesn't need to be texted about. But on the average it's true. I would want to be allowed to say "I'm okay" without it being heard as either reassurance or defiance. Just a status report.

The version that would actually feel like the truth, then, is probably not a version I can give to anyone in a single conversation. It might be a version I can only give in pieces, over years, to the few people who are still going to be around to hear the long form. Jamie, who already knows it. Theo and Nina eventually, when they're old enough to be curious about what their dad did with the middle of his life. Maybe Dani, if Dani ever asks the real question instead of the careful one. Maybe a future version of myself who reads this back and finds it either embarrassing or accurate, possibly both.

For now, when Mom calls this afternoon, I will probably say something gestural. I will say I'm working on a few things, that there's a possible project, that we'll see. And I will hang up and stand in the kitchen for a minute feeling the gap between what I said and what is true, and I will let the gap be there, because closing it has costs I am not willing to pay yet. The dog will come over. The kettle will be there. That's the truthful version. It just doesn't fit on a phone call.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of question people ask — "so what are you up to these days?" — that's been landing differently lately. Describe the last time someone asked you some version of it. Where were you, who was asking, and what did you actually say versus what was running underneath?
entries.content:
Mom called Tuesday evening, around 7:15, which is her window — after her dinner, before whatever she watches at eight. I was loading the dishwasher and Jamie was at the table going through what looked like a stack of mail that had been accumulating for a week. I picked up because I had not picked up Sunday and the guilt had a half-life of about forty-eight hours.

She did the opening — the weather where she is, a thing about the neighbor's dog, a small report on her knee — and then she did the thing she does, which is to slide the question in sideways, embedded in something else, so it sounds like she just thought of it. "And how about you, sweetheart, are you keeping busy with everything?"

Keeping busy with everything. The word "everything" doing an enormous amount of work there, because there is no everything. There is a stack of paper on the kitchen table, there is the printout from Pat under a school form, there is a half-drafted document I have not opened in nine days. "Everything" is a generous container she is offering me to put a respectable noun into.

What I said was: "Yeah, you know, I've got a few things going. I had coffee with Pat last week, we're talking through some possibilities. And I've been doing a lot of reading. It's been a good month."

Five sentences. None of them lies, exactly. All of them shaped to land in the receiver of a 76-year-old woman in a quiet house an hour away who I love and who I do not, at 7:15 on a Tuesday, have the energy to give the long version to.

What was running underneath was: Mom, I do not have a job. I do not have a project. I have a printout under a school form and an agreement with another adult to not do anything for a month. I have a kettle and a dog and a stack of books and a working hypothesis that the answer will not arrive as a noun. I am asking you to trust me through a stretch where I cannot fully vouch for myself, and I know that is a hard thing to ask of a parent, especially the parent who is left, especially when the parent who is left was married to the man who used to say you could tell what someone did for a living by how they answered the question at a barbecue. I know what you are checking on when you ask. You are checking on whether I have made a mistake. And I cannot give you the answer you want, which is "no," because I do not know the answer yet, and I will not know it for a while.

What I said instead was: "It's been a good month."

And she said: "Well. As long as you're feeling okay about it." Which is the line she has been using since around February. *Feeling okay about it.* Like the it is a decision I might still be appraising, like there is a window in which I might revise. I don't think she means it as pressure. I think she genuinely cannot quite tell from this distance whether I am fine or whether I am quietly coming apart, and the question is her way of taking a temperature without saying she is taking one. If I said "actually, no, I'm not feeling okay about it," I think she would mobilize. I think she would call Dani. I think she would offer to come stay for a weekend. The supportiveness is real. It's just that it activates in a register I would rather not summon.

So I said I was feeling okay about it, which I mostly was, sitting there with a wet sponge in my other hand, the dishwasher half-loaded, Jamie at the table not looking up but listening, the way she does. And then Mom did her closing, which is a list of small updates that don't require a response — something about her book club, something about a cousin I haven't seen in a decade — and we said our I love yous and hung up.

I stood in the kitchen for a minute, exactly the way I said I would in the last thing I wrote. The gap was there. Jamie looked up and said, "Your mom?" and I said, "Yeah," and she said, "How is she?" and I said, "Good, I think." And that was the whole exchange. Jamie went back to the mail. I went back to the dishwasher. The dog had not moved.

What I keep coming back to is the difference between what Mom is asking and what Marcus was asking and what Pat is asking, which are three different questions that all arrive in the same wrapper. Marcus wanted a category — sabbatical, breakdown, reinvention — so he could file me. Mom wants a reassurance — that the man she raised has not lost his footing. Pat wants the actual answer, because Pat is on the same road and is using my report to triangulate. Three completely different requests. One sentence shape. *So what are you up to.*

And I have been treating them as if they were the same question and trying to find one answer that works for all three, which is why every answer feels slightly false. The Marcus answer should be a category, gracefully delivered, and then I should let him go to dairy. The Mom answer should be a reassurance, lovingly delivered, even when I cannot fully back it. The Pat answer is the one I owe the truth to, and the only one of the three where the long version actually fits.

I think I have been giving Mom the wrong answer for nine months. Not because I have been lying. Because I have been giving her something close to the Pat answer in the time allotted for the Mom answer, and it comes out as neither — it comes out as a little weather report of vagueness that I think reads, on her end, as evasion. She is hearing me hedge, and she is interpreting the hedge as worry, and so she calls more, which gives me more chances to hedge, which gives her more reason to worry. We have built a small machine.

I could just say: Mom, I'm okay. I'm taking my time on purpose. I'll tell you when there's news. I love you.

I could say that. I might, next time. I'll see how Tuesday at 7:15 feels.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you got irritated at someone in your house over something that, looking back, wasn't really about that thing? Describe the moment — what they did, what you said or didn't say, and what you think was actually underneath it.
entries.content:
Wednesday night Theo left his backpack in the middle of the kitchen floor again. Not on the bench by the door, where the bench exists explicitly for the backpack. Not on the chair, which would have been at least gestural toward putting-away. The middle of the floor, with one strap splayed out like the bag had been shot. I came around the island carrying the colander and almost stepped on it, did the small dance you do when you're holding hot pasta water, and said — too loud, with too much edge — "Theo. The backpack. Come on, man."

He was already at the table on his phone. He looked up with that particular fourteen-year-old expression that is somehow both injured and bored, said "sorry, geez," got up, picked up the bag, dropped it on the bench from a foot above so it made a thud that was a sentence, and sat back down. Nina, who misses nothing, said "Dad's mad about the backpack." Jamie didn't look up but I could feel her not looking up, which is its own kind of looking.

The thing is, the backpack was the backpack. It wasn't the worst version of the backpack. He hadn't tracked anything in. He hadn't lied about homework. He had walked in from school and put his bag down and gotten a snack and started his phone. Standard operating procedure for a fourteen-year-old. And I had snapped at him like he had done something.

I was quiet through dinner. Theo was quieter, which is saying something. Nina filled the air, which is her job in this house and one I am grateful for even when she's repeating a thing about a YouTube person I don't understand. Jamie looked at me across the table once with a face I can't quite describe — not concerned, not annoyed, just sort of *I see you* — and went back to her plate. After, I cleared, and Theo took the dog out without being asked, which I think was a peace offering, or possibly an escape, possibly both.

When I sat down later with a beer and the dishwasher running, what I actually was, which I had not let myself name for about three hours, was that I had spent most of the afternoon in the half-drafted document. Not writing in it. Looking at it. Making a small change, undoing the small change. Reading something Pat had sent me a link to and not knowing what to do with it. At about 4:15 I had closed the laptop and stood up and felt that very specific kind of full-body uselessness that is hard to describe to anyone who hasn't done it, where you have not technically wasted the day but you also could not, under oath, account for it. I had gone to start dinner because dinner was a thing I could finish.

And then Theo's backpack was on the floor.

What was actually underneath it, I think, was: somebody in this house should be doing what they are supposed to do. Somebody should be operating according to the visible rules. The bench is for the bag. School is for school. A job is for a paycheck. The world used to work in this clean way and now it doesn't, at least not for me, and here is my son not even doing the small clean version of it that is available to him, which is *putting the bag on the bench.* And so the irritation had a target it didn't deserve. Theo was just the nearest piece of disorder.

That's the unflattering version. The slightly more flattering one is that I have been keyed up all week about the document I am not writing and the call I owe Chris that I keep not making and the fact that next Tuesday is going to be another 7:15 phone call with Mom and I have not yet decided whether I am going to give her the cleaner answer I half-promised myself I would. All of that pressure was sitting in my chest at low volume all afternoon, and a backpack in the wrong place was the thing that took the lid off.

Either way: Theo did not deserve the tone. The tone was for me.

I went up to his room around nine. He was on the bed with the lamp on, headphones on, doing whatever he does. I knocked on the doorframe and he pulled one earbud out. I said, "Hey. About earlier. The backpack thing — I was sharper than I needed to be. It wasn't really about the bag." He looked at me for a second, weighing it, and said, "Okay." Just okay. And then, after a beat, "I'll put it on the bench." And I said, "I appreciate that, but I'm saying it wasn't actually about the bench." And he said, "I know." Which surprised me. *I know.* Like he had already done the math himself and was just waiting to see if I would.

I said goodnight and went back downstairs and Jamie was on the couch with a book and she said, without looking up, "Did you go talk to him?" and I said yes, and she said "Good," and turned the page. That was all of it. The whole exchange about the whole evening, four words.

What I keep coming back to is that *I know* from Theo. Because it means he sees more than the quiet suggests. It means he has noticed his father is keyed up about something that is not him, and has decided, in his own fourteen-year-old way, not to make it harder. Which is generous of him. More generous than I was being. And it makes me wonder what else he has been quietly noticing this year that I have been assuming he was too inside his own thing to see.

I am going to put my own bag on the bench tomorrow. Not as a gesture. Just because the bench is for the bag.

entries.prompt: How are you spending money differently now than you were a year ago, and what does the change in your spending say that you maybe haven't said out loud?
entries.content:
The credit card statement came in the mail yesterday — physical, because I have not gotten around to switching it to paperless, which is itself a small data point — and I sat at the kitchen table after Jamie went to bed and went through it line by line with a pen, the way my father used to do, which I had not done in probably fifteen years. When I had a salary I just paid the thing. Now I am the kind of person who circles items and writes small notations in the margin. I am becoming someone I would have found slightly tragic at thirty-five, and I do not mind it as much as I would have predicted.

The biggest visible change is the lunches. A year ago I was spending — I went back and looked, because I am that person now — somewhere between three hundred and four hundred dollars a month on lunches and coffees and the small transactional purchases of a working day. The salad place near the office. The coffee on the way in and the coffee at three. The dinner out when something ran late. All of that is just gone. Zero. In its place is groceries, which are up, but not by anywhere near the amount the lunches are down. It turns out a sandwich made at home costs about a dollar fifty, which I knew abstractly and had simply chosen not to think about for two decades. I was effectively paying a tax of around three thousand dollars a year for the privilege of not making a sandwich. That's the math I would not have wanted to do while I was doing it.

The clothes are the other obvious one. I have not bought a piece of work clothing in nine months. No shirts, no shoes, nothing dry-cleaned. The dry-cleaning line on the statement, which used to be a steady drumbeat, just stops in February. I noticed it and felt something I want to be careful about naming. Not pride exactly. More like the small clean feeling of a thing no longer happening. The closet in the bedroom has the same shirts in it that were in it in February, and they are fine, and nobody has commented, because nobody was actually grading me on them in the way I half-believed they were.

What's gone up: groceries, as I said. Books, by a noticeable amount — I have been buying used mostly, but still, there is a pile next to the bed that represents real money. The heating bill, because I am home during the day now and I am not willing to sit in a forty-eight-degree house to prove a point. Vet, because the dog is older. Gas is down because I am not commuting. The wine line is roughly flat, which I find funny and will not investigate further.

What is more interesting than the categories is the rhythm. I used to spend without thinking and reconcile once a quarter when something looked off. Now I notice every thirty-dollar charge. Not in a panicked way — we are not in trouble — but in a way where the number registers. Jamie ordered something for Nina last week, a thing for school, and I saw the charge on the joint card and felt, for half a second, a small contraction in my chest before I read what it was for and let it go. That contraction is new. That contraction is the eleven-p.m.-spreadsheet voice arriving at three in the afternoon to say hello.

The spreadsheet, by the way, is real. I have a spreadsheet now. I built it in March and I update it on Sunday nights when the house is quiet. It has a tab for the runway, which is what I am calling it, and the runway tab tells me, given current burn, how many months we have before the math gets uncomfortable. The number was twenty-two when I built it. It is now fourteen. I know this without looking. It is in my head the way phone numbers used to be.

What the change in spending says, that I have not said out loud, is something like: I am quietly preparing for a version of this where it doesn't work. Not predicting it. Preparing for it. The lunches were the first thing because the lunches were the easiest, but underneath the lunches is a slow, unannounced reorganization of how this household runs, conducted mostly by me, mostly at the kitchen table after everyone else is asleep. I have not told Jamie that I am tracking things this closely, because Jamie would say it is fine, we are fine, and Jamie would be right in the short term and possibly not right in the long term, and I would rather be the one carrying that uncertainty than ask her to carry half of it before it is necessary. Which is its own kind of decision, and not a fully honest one, and I notice myself making it.

The other thing it says, which is harder, is that I am beginning to want less. Not in a virtuous way. In a sort of disinterested way. The things I used to buy without thinking — the third pair of shoes, the gadget, the upgrade — I look at them now and they do not turn me on the way they used to. I do not know if this is genuine or if it is the ego protecting itself by deciding the grapes were sour. Probably some of both. But there is a version of the last nine months where the most significant thing that has happened is that I stopped wanting a number of things I used to want, and I am not sure what to do with that, because the wanting was part of how I knew I was alive.

What I would like to want, I think, is the time. To be willing to spend money on the time, when it comes to that — to pay for another six months of this if it's needed, without flinching, without the eleven-p.m. voice. To treat the runway tab not as a countdown but as a budget for an unnamed thing I am buying. The unnamed thing being, I suppose, this. Whatever this turns out to be.

I put the statement in the drawer. The dog made her noise. I went to bed.

entries.prompt: What's a small thing you've been telling yourself you'll get to — not a big project, just something on the edge of your attention — and what happens in the moment you remember it and then don't do it?
entries.content:
The thing is the garage light. The bulb in the fixture over the side door, the one you hit with the switch when you come in from the driveway with groceries, has been out since — I want to say August. Maybe late July. Long enough that I have stopped reaching for the switch on the way in and have started, without thinking about it, angling my body to use the light from the kitchen door instead. The body has adapted. That's how I know it's been a while. When the body adapts, you have crossed from "I need to fix that" to "this is how the house is now."

It is a ten-minute job. Less. It is a stepladder, which is in the garage, and a bulb, which I would have to buy, which is the only actual obstacle, and even that obstacle is undone by the fact that I am in the hardware store roughly every other week for some other reason. I have been in that hardware store six or seven times since the bulb went out. I have walked past the aisle with the bulbs in it. I have, on at least two occasions, stood in the aisle adjacent to it. I have not bought the bulb.

What happens, when I remember it, is small and consistent enough that I can almost narrate it. I will be coming in from the driveway with a bag in each hand, it will be after five and getting dark earlier than it was a week ago, I will reach for the switch out of muscle memory I apparently still have despite the adaptation, the switch will do nothing, and I will think, oh right, the bulb. And then in the next half second I will think, I should pick one up tomorrow. And in the half second after that I will be in the kitchen putting the bag down and Nina will be saying something or the dog will be doing her greeting and the thought will go where the thought goes, which is wherever unfiled thoughts go in this house, which is apparently the same place as the document I am not writing and the call I owe Chris.

What I notice is that the not-doing is not a decision. It does not feel like a decision. It feels like a small piece of weather. The thought arrives, the thought departs, I am not even the one moving it. If I were actively choosing not to fix the light, I think I would feel something about it — guilt, or stubbornness, or the small pleasure of refusal. I feel none of those. I feel the way you feel about a thing that is happening on a channel you are not watching.

And yet the bulb is, I think, doing some work. Because when I sat down to write this and tried to think of the small thing I have been not getting to, the bulb was the first thing that came up, ahead of probably twenty more substantial things, and it came up with a small charge on it. Some warmth, some heat, I don't know. Enough that I am writing about it instead of about the document or the call.

I think what the bulb is, is a test I set for myself without meaning to. Not a test of whether I am capable of buying a lightbulb. I am capable of buying a lightbulb. The test is whether the small upkeeps of this life, the maintenance gestures that used to happen in the margins of a working week, will continue to happen now that the week has no margins because the week has no center. When I had the job, the bulb would have been replaced within four days. Not because I was more competent then. Because the structure of the week threw off enough momentum that small things got swept up in it. You were already running errands on Saturday because Saturday was the only day for errands. You bought the bulb on the way to the other thing.

Now there is no other thing. Saturday is not the only day for errands. Saturday is a day like any other day, which means the bulb can be bought on any of seven days, which means it gets bought on none of them, because the absence of a deadline turns out to be, for me, the same as the absence of a reason. This is the part I would not have predicted. I assumed, when I left, that I would become more efficient at the small stuff because I would have more room for it. The opposite has happened. The small stuff has expanded to fill whatever container I give it, except for the containers it refuses to enter, which include the bulb.

There are other things on the same shelf. The tire that is probably a little low and that I have been meaning to put air in for three weeks. The book Chris lent me that I finished a month ago and have not returned. A button on a coat. The list goes on and the list is, I notice, all physical. It is all things that would require me to stand up and move my body in a specific direction toward a specific small task. The non-physical small things — the email reply, the form for Nina's school — those I do, because those I can do from this same chair. The bulb requires the chair to be left.

I think what happens in the moment I remember the bulb and don't do it is that I get a tiny preview of the larger pattern, and I look away from it, the way you look away from a photo of yourself you don't like. The pattern being: there is a version of this where the days fill with thinking and reading and small electronic gestures and the actual house, the actual yard, the actual car, the actual body, slowly drift into a state of quiet disrepair, and one day Jamie comes in from the driveway and reaches for the switch and looks at me and the look is not angry, it is something else, and I have to answer for the bulb and for everything the bulb stands in for.

That is overstated. The bulb is a bulb. But also it isn't, quite, or I would have bought it.

I am going to go to the hardware store this afternoon. I am writing that down so it is harder to not do. A 60-watt, the kind that fits the fixture, which I will check before I go by actually walking out to the garage and unscrewing the dead one and bringing it with me, because that is how a person who is not pretending does it.

If I do not do this today, I will write about that next time, and I will have to be honest about why.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a recent conversation where you found yourself performing patience — staying even-toned on the outside while something else was happening underneath. What was the conversation, and what was the gap between the two?
entries.content:
Saturday morning, the kitchen, Nina at the island with a bowl of cereal and her tablet, asking me to explain compound interest. Not for school. For some video she had watched where a person in a backwards cap was telling teenagers they were behind already if they hadn't started investing. She wanted to know, specifically, whether it was true that she was behind. Eleven years old. Backwards cap.

I poured another coffee and sat down across from her and started in on the explanation, which I am good at, or used to be good at, the kind of explanation that involves drawing little curves in the air and using the word "essentially." I did the thing about a dollar today being worth more than a dollar tomorrow. I did the thing about the curve getting steeper. I drew, on the back of a school flyer that was lying on the counter, the little hockey-stick graph that everyone draws. I kept my voice in the register I keep my voice in when I am explaining something to one of the kids, which is patient and slightly warm, the register of a person who has all the time in the world for this question.

What was happening underneath was that I wanted to take the tablet and throw it across the room.

Not at Nina. At the person in the backwards cap. At whatever algorithm had served him to my eleven-year-old on a Saturday morning. At the entire apparatus that had decided the appropriate thing to tell a child was that she was already behind. Behind what. Behind whom. There is a version of me, the version with the salary and the slide decks, who would have nodded along with the backwards-cap guy in some abstract way, who would have agreed that yes, of course, start early, the magic of compounding, the responsible thing. And there is the version of me sitting at the kitchen table in November having just spent Sunday night looking at the runway tab, who heard the question from Nina and felt something hot and protective rise up that had very little to do with finance.

I kept explaining. I drew the curve. I said the word "essentially." Nina nodded the way she nods, which is to say partially, and asked a follow-up that was sharper than I expected, about whether you could lose money doing it, and I said yes, you could, and that was part of it, and the curve was not a guarantee, it was a tendency, and tendencies were not the same as promises. She said "okay" in a way that suggested she would be returning to the backwards-cap guy with my answer to evaluate it.

What I wanted to say, and did not, was something like: the man in the video is selling you a feeling. He is selling you the feeling of being ahead, which is the same feeling he is selling the thirty-year-olds and the forty-year-olds, and the feeling does not go away when you achieve the thing, it just relocates. There is no arrival. There is a curve, and the curve is real, but standing on the curve does not feel the way the video makes it look. I know because I stood on a version of it for twenty years and the feeling I had was mostly that I was about to fall off. I would like for you to not spend your eleven-year-old Saturday morning being recruited into a feeling I am still trying to put down at forty-seven.

I did not say any of that. Because Nina had asked a question about compound interest and the answer to a question about compound interest is not a speech about the psychology of striving. Because she is eleven and I am her father and the appropriate response to "explain this to me" is to explain it, not to perform my own complicated relationship to it on top of her question like a second sandwich she did not order. So I performed patience. The patience was real in its content — I do have time for her question, I do want to answer it well — and false in its evenness, because underneath the evenness was a current of something close to anger that had nothing to do with her.

She finished her cereal. She said thanks. She took the tablet into the living room. I sat at the island for a minute looking at the hockey-stick graph I had drawn on the back of the flyer, which was actually pretty good, a clean little curve, and I felt the gap between what I had said and what I had not said and tried to decide whether the gap was a failure of honesty or a small, appropriate piece of parenting. Probably both. Probably the job is to give her the curve and not the speech, and to give her the speech in pieces over years, when she is older and has asked a different kind of question.

What I keep coming back to is that the patience I performed was not for Nina. Nina did not need me to be patient. Nina was being perfectly reasonable. The patience was for the version of me that wanted to throw the tablet, and the patience kept that version in the chair, drawing the curve, using the word "essentially." Which is, I suppose, what patience often is — not a feeling you have but a thing you do to manage a feeling you have. The even tone is the leash.

Later, when Jamie came down, I told her about it, just the surface of it — Nina asked about compound interest, I drew her a graph, she seemed satisfied. Jamie said, "She's been watching those videos again." I said, "Yeah." And we left it there, which was the right amount, and the dog watched us from the rug, and I put the flyer in the recycling, curve and all.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you laughed — really laughed, not the polite kind — and what was going on around it? Tell me where you were, who you were with, and what set it off.
entries.content:
Friday night, the four of us at the table, and the thing that set it off was the dog.

We were eating that sheet-pan chicken-and-vegetable thing Jamie does, which is the meal we land on when nobody has the energy to plan a meal but everyone agrees we should not order pizza again. Nina was telling a story — a long one, with multiple acts — about a girl in her grade who had attempted to bring a hamster to school in a sock. The sock detail was important to her. She kept returning to it. "In a *sock*, Mom. Like a regular sock." Theo was eating the way he eats now, which is methodically and without commentary, but he was listening, I could tell, because his shoulders were doing the thing where they're slightly turned toward whoever is talking even when his face isn't.

The dog, meanwhile, had been doing her under-the-table circuit, which is the slow patrol where she checks each set of feet for the possibility of fallen chicken. She had come around to my side and parked herself with her chin on my knee, which is her opening move, the one that says *I see you and I am available for any food you do not want.* I was not paying attention to her. I was listening to Nina and the sock.

What I did not see, because I was looking at Nina, was Jamie reach down with a piece of carrot, intending to give it to the dog under the table, the way she does sometimes when she thinks I'm not watching, even though we have an explicit no-feeding-from-the-table rule that she herself instituted in 2019. The dog, who had been positioned at my knee, saw the carrot coming from Jamie's side and lunged, and in lunging, knocked her head into the underside of the table hard enough that two glasses of water jumped, and then she let out a sound — not a yelp, not a bark, something between a cough and a sneeze, a single sharp *HUH* — and sat back on her haunches with an expression of pure betrayed dignity, like the table had insulted her personally.

There was a half-second of silence where all four of us looked at the dog and the dog looked at the table and the table sat there blamelessly, and then Nina lost it. Just absolutely lost it. The kind of laugh that starts as a snort and then becomes a wheeze and then becomes a thing where she has to put her head down on the table because her body cannot hold it. And Theo — Theo, who has been a quiet shoulders-only presence at meals for about eight months — Theo started laughing too, hard, with his whole face, the way he used to laugh when he was nine and we used to do the bit where I would pretend to forget his name. I had not heard that laugh in I don't know how long. Months. And Jamie, who had been caught carrot-handed, started laughing in the guilty way of a person who has been busted, and the dog, who had now recovered her composure, looked around at the four of us and gave a single tail-thump on the floor like *yes, I agree, that was a lot*, and then I went, too.

I went hard. The kind of laughing where you can't get a breath in cleanly, where every time you almost recover you make eye contact with someone and it starts again. Nina kept saying "her *face*" and dissolving. Theo did an imitation of the *HUH* sound that was almost exactly right, and that set Nina off again, and that set me off again. At one point I had tears, actual tears, running down. I had to take my glasses off. I have not laughed like that in — I genuinely cannot remember. Maybe a year. Maybe more.

What I noticed, after, when we had all calmed down and were back to the chicken and Nina was returning, with effort, to the hamster-in-a-sock story, was that I felt physically different. Lighter in the shoulders. The thing in my chest that has been at low volume since roughly March, the small persistent compression of it, was gone for about ten minutes. Not managed. Not addressed. Gone. The way a headache is gone when it goes — you don't notice the absence at first because you had stopped noticing the presence.

And the second thing I noticed, which I am still turning over, is that all four of us laughed. Not three of us with the fourth performing along. All four. Theo, who has been somewhere else for the better part of a year, was right here, head back, laughing at his sister's laugh and then at the dog's face and then at his mother's guilt. He had not crossed some bridge to come back. He had simply been findable, the whole time, by something stupid enough. The bar was a dog hitting her head on a table. That was the bar. Nothing about my year of careful presence and reading more and being available had produced this. A carrot had produced this.

I think what is sitting with me from it is that I have been treating my relationship to the kids, and to this whole stretch, as a kind of project — a thing I am working on, a thing I am being deliberate about. And the project frame is not wrong, exactly, but the project frame would never have produced Friday night. Friday night was produced by the four of us being in the same room with a hungry dog and a piece of carrot and no agenda. The project does not know how to do that. The project, in fact, would probably have gotten in the way of it, because the project would have had me watching for the moment instead of being inside it.

After dinner Theo loaded the dishwasher without being asked, which is becoming a small pattern I am trying not to comment on in case commenting breaks it. Nina retold the story to Jamie as if Jamie had not been there. The dog ate her actual dinner from her actual bowl and did not appear to remember the table incident at all, or had decided to forgive it. Jamie caught my eye over the sink and we did the small smile that is the shorthand we have built up, the one that means *that was a good one, that was a real one,* and went back to the dishes.

I want more of those. I cannot make more of those. I can only be in the room.

entries.prompt: Think about something you used to do regularly — a habit, a hobby, a small ritual — that's quietly fallen out of your life. When did you last do it, and what's filled the space it used to take up?
entries.content:
The Sunday paper. That's the one. I have not bought a Sunday paper since — I want to say March, maybe February. There was a stretch in my twenties and thirties and most of my forties where Sunday morning meant walking down to the place on the corner, when we lived in the place that had a corner, and later driving to the gas station that carried it, and coming home with the paper and a coffee and putting the paper on the kitchen table and slowly going through it. Not reading it cover to cover. Going through it. The order mattered. The front section first but only the headlines. Then the magazine. Then the book review, which was the section I cared about most and saved like dessert. Then the rest, in a sequence I could not articulate but could feel.

I did this for twenty-some years. Through the apartment, through the first house, through this house. Through both kids being infants. Through the year I traveled too much and was only home on weekends and the paper became the thing that proved I was a person with a Sunday and not just a person with a calendar. Jamie used to take the styles section, which I did not read, and I would take everything else, and we would sit at the same table with our two halves of the same object, drinking coffee, not talking much, the kids eventually awake and in the room making noise around us, and that was Sunday.

I cannot tell you when I stopped. There was no decision. I think there was a Sunday in February or early March where I did not go get it because I was not going to the gas station for any other reason — I was not commuting, I was not picking anything up — and the trip just to get the paper felt suddenly absurd, an artifact of a life in which I was always already going somewhere. And then there was the next Sunday, and I did not go get it then either, and after about three weeks of not going, the not-going became the thing, and I never started again.

What's filled the space, if I am being honest, is the phone. Which is the answer I do not want to give but is the true one. I get up earlier than I used to, before everyone else, with the dog, with the kettle, and I read for forty minutes — I have written about that, I have been almost smug about that — but the forty minutes is books, and the books happen *first*, and what happens *after* the forty minutes, in the slot the paper used to occupy, is that I sit on the couch with the second coffee and I scroll. News sites, mostly. The same five or six. I refresh them. I read a piece halfway. I open another tab. I check email even though no one emails me on Sunday morning. I look at the weather for the week. I check the same news site again, and there is nothing new on it because it has been eight minutes. This goes on for an hour, sometimes longer. By the time Jamie comes down I have absorbed nothing and could not name a single article I read.

The paper used to take about the same amount of time. Maybe a little more. But the paper had a shape. The paper ended. You finished the book review and you put the section down and you were done with the book review. You did not refresh the book review. The paper had edges, and inside the edges was a finite amount of stuff, and when you were done with the stuff you were done, and Sunday continued. The phone has no edges. The phone does not end. The phone is always slightly more available than it was a minute ago, and I have been giving it the slot that used to belong to a thing with edges.

What I notice, sitting here writing this, is that I miss the paper in a specific way that is not really about the paper. I miss being in possession of a finite object on a Sunday morning. I miss the small ceremony of it — the walking down, the coming back, the spreading it out, the noise it made when you turned the pages, which is a noise the kids will probably not associate with Sunday the way I do. I miss that Jamie and I were doing a thing, in parallel, that was the same thing. We do not have a parallel thing on Sunday morning anymore. She does her crossword on her phone and I do my doom-scroll on mine and we are technically in the same room but we are each in our own private informational weather, and there is no shared object on the table between us.

The paper, I should say, still exists. The gas station still carries it. I could buy one this Sunday. I almost want to write that I am going to, the way I wrote about the bulb, but I am suspicious of myself when I make those promises in this document, because the bulb did get fixed — I did go to the hardware store, I did unscrew the dead one and bring it with me, the new one is in and the side door is illuminated again — but I notice I am keeping the bulb in mind as a small piece of evidence I can produce if questioned, and I do not want the paper to become evidence. I want the paper to become Sunday again, or I want to admit it isn't going to, and either of those is fine, but the in-between thing where I gesture at it and don't do it is the thing I'm trying to do less of.

So I will say this instead. Next Sunday I am going to leave the phone upstairs when I come down for coffee. That is the smaller, more honest move. I do not need the paper to come back. I need the slot to not be the phone. If the slot is empty for a while, that is okay. The slot was empty when I was a kid on Sunday mornings and nothing bad happened. The dog will be there. The kettle will be there. Maybe Jamie will come down and we will not have a parallel thing and that will be fine, or maybe one of us will start something — a crossword on actual paper, who knows. Small. I am keeping it small. The point is the slot.

entries.prompt: Where in your work life right now do you feel most like you're going through the motions? Pick a specific recent task or meeting and walk me through it — what you did, what you were thinking while you did it, and what you'd rather have been doing.
entries.content:
Tuesday afternoon I sat down at the kitchen table with the laptop and updated my LinkedIn profile. That is the task. I want to be precise about it because the act itself was almost embarrassingly small and the ninety minutes I spent on it were, looking back, the most clearly going-through-the-motions ninety minutes I have spent in a while.

Here is what I did. I changed the headline, which had been the old job title with the company name attached, to something I had been workshopping in a notes file for about two months — a phrase I will not write here because writing it would commit it to a permanence I do not yet feel — involving the word "advisor" and the word "transitions" and a verb I changed three times. I added an end date to the old role, which I had been avoiding because adding an end date makes the gap visible, and the gap is now nine months long, and there is no graceful way to render nine months as anything other than nine months. I considered, for a long minute, adding a new "current" entry that would say something gestural about independent work, the way other people do, the kind of entry that is mostly a placeholder dressed up as a development. I did not add it. Then I almost added it. Then I closed the tab. Then I reopened the tab and adjusted the headline one more time, swapping the verb back to the verb I had started with.

What I was thinking, while I did this, was a kind of low-grade humming dread mixed with a sort of clinical detachment, like I was filling out a form for a person I used to know. I kept thinking about who would see the update. Marcus would see it, presumably, because Marcus is on there, and the update would arrive in his feed as a small notification and he would do, at his desk in his quarter-zip, the same face he did in the cereal aisle. Old colleagues would see it. The senior person from the 2014 meeting whose nod I had been carrying around like a relic — that person is on there too, and would see it, and would form some quiet opinion that I would never know the contents of and would nonetheless be performing for. I was, in other words, writing a single line of text for an audience of perhaps four hundred people I have not spoken to in a year, most of whom would scroll past it in under a second, and I was treating it as if it were a press release.

The thing I kept doing, which I want to be honest about, was previewing. There is a button — or maybe just a sense of one — where you can see how your profile will look to someone else, and I kept toggling to it, reading my own headline as if I were Marcus or the senior person or Pat, trying to feel how it would land, then going back to edit. I did this maybe twelve times. The headline did not get materially better between toggle six and toggle twelve. It just got slightly different in ways only I could see, the way a sentence you have read aloud thirty times stops being a sentence and becomes a sequence of phonemes. By the end I could not tell if the headline was good or bad. I could only tell that I had spent ninety minutes on it and that my coffee had gone cold and that I had not, in any real sense, done any work. I had performed the appearance of having work to put a label on.

What I was actually doing, underneath, was trying to manufacture a noun. The whole exercise was the noun-wanting part of me running a small, quiet errand on its own behalf while the rest of me was distracted. I had not sat down intending to update LinkedIn. I had sat down intending to open the half-drafted document, the one I have not opened in — I checked, because I have become this kind of person — sixteen days now. I opened the laptop, I looked at the document icon, I felt the small refusal, and I navigated, almost without choosing to, to the browser, and from the browser to the profile, and the profile is a place where it is possible to feel as if you are doing something professional without doing anything at all. It is a slot machine for that feeling. You pull the lever, you adjust a word, you preview, you adjust another word, and the dopamine of having-acted is dispensed in small reliable doses, and at the end of an hour and a half you have moved no actual piece of your actual situation forward by any measurable amount.

What I would rather have been doing, if I am being honest, is the document. Not because the document is going to save me. The document might be nothing. The document might be the thing under the school form a year from now, with coffee rings on it. But the document is the only piece of this I have not yet performed for an audience, and the LinkedIn update was, in its small way, a performance to avoid the unwitnessed thing. The document is unwitnessed. Nobody has asked about it. Pat does not know it exists. If I worked on it for two hours and the two hours were bad, no one would ever know, which is exactly what makes it hard to start and exactly what makes it the thing I should be starting.

What I would *also* rather have been doing, in a different register, is nothing. Just sitting. The afternoon was clear and cold, the kind of November light I usually like, and I could have taken the dog out and walked the loop around the back streets and let the not-doing be honest not-doing, instead of the dressed-up not-doing of the profile edit. The dressed-up version is worse. The dressed-up version eats the same time and leaves a residue. Honest not-doing leaves you where you were. Performed working leaves you slightly behind, because you have spent your attention and gotten a worse version of rest.

I closed the laptop around three thirty. I did not publish the update. The headline reverted to whatever it had been before, because I had never hit save. Ninety minutes, no artifact, and a vague sense of having been busy that I am now, sitting here, declining to accept. The dog was at the door. I took her out. The light was already going.

entries.prompt: Is there someone you've been meaning to reach out to but haven't? Describe the last time you thought about doing it — where you were, what stopped you, and what you ended up doing instead.
entries.content:
Chris. I have been meaning to call Chris for — I want to say six weeks, though if I am honest with the calendar it is closer to eight. He texted me in mid-September with a link to something he thought I would find interesting, an article about a guy from our old industry who had done a podcast about leaving, and I read the article and I did not respond. Then a week later he sent a follow-up that was just *anyway, hope you're well, let's catch up sometime,* and I responded to that one with a thumbs-up reaction, which is the least a person can do without doing nothing, and then I did nothing. That is where it has been sitting since.

The last time I thought about it specifically was last Thursday, on a walk. I had taken the dog out the long way, the loop that goes past the elementary school and around behind the church, and I was about halfway when I realized I had been having an entire conversation with Chris in my head for about ten minutes without noticing. Not rehearsing — that is the Pat move, and the Mom move, and apparently a lot of my moves — but just talking to him, the way you do with someone you used to talk to without effort. I was telling him, internally, about the laughing at dinner with the dog and the table, and I could hear him laughing in the specific way he laughs, which is mostly through his nose, and I thought, oh. I should call him. I should actually call him. And I took my phone out of my pocket and I held it for about a block and I did not call him, and I put it back.

What stopped me was not what I would have predicted, which is that I would feel I owed him an account of myself and didn't have one ready. That is the version I would have written down a few months ago. The actual thing that stopped me, standing there on the corner by the church with the dog pulling toward a squirrel, was something more specific and harder to admit. It was that Chris is the last person from that life who still calls me, and as long as I have not called him back, the line is open. The minute I call him, the call happens, and the call will be good or it will be awkward, and if it is awkward in a particular way — if there is a moment where he is being kind in the register of someone still on the thing, the way Marcus was kind in the cereal aisle — then I will know something I do not currently know, which is whether Chris is a person from before or a person from now. Right now he is in a kind of superposition. He is both. As long as I do not call him, he stays both.

I put the phone back and the dog and I finished the loop and I came home and unloaded the dishwasher and that was it. I did not even feel guilty about not calling, which is itself a sign of something. Guilt would mean I had decided I should have. The absence of guilt means I had let it slide back into the not-quite-decision space where most of these things live for me now.

What I ended up doing instead, that evening, was rereading the article he sent. The whole thing this time, slowly, with my coffee. The guy in the article had left two years before me and was clearly further along in figuring out what came next, and I read it the way you read a postcard from a friend who got to the destination first — with interest, and a little envy, and a little reassurance that the destination exists. And then I noticed I was reading an article a friend had sent me as a substitute for talking to the friend, and I closed the tab, and I sat there for a minute feeling caught.

The thing I want to be honest about is that Chris has done nothing wrong. Chris has been kind. The two texts were exactly the right amount of pressure — present, undemanding, the texts of a person who would be glad to talk and is not going to make me feel bad if I don't. He has not, as far as I can tell, written me off. He is just waiting. And the longer I wait, the more the not-calling becomes a thing in itself, a small accumulating debt, and I know how this goes, because I have done it before, with other people, in other stretches — at some point the debt gets large enough that the call becomes its own event, an apology call, a where-have-you-been call, and the size of the call becomes another reason not to make it, and eventually the friendship is just the friendship that ended without anyone deciding it should.

I do not want that with Chris. I have been telling myself I do not want it and then behaving as if I am willing to risk it, which is the gap I keep finding in this stretch — between the things I say I value and the small daily votes I cast against them.

Here is what I think I am going to do. Not call him. Calling him is too big right now and I will not do it and then I will write about not doing it and we will be in the bulb-pattern, which I am trying to be done with. I am going to text him back, today, with actual words. Not a thumbs-up. Something like: *been quiet, sorry, the article was good, want to catch up on a walk sometime in the next couple weeks.* Six clauses, give or take. The last clause is the one that matters because it commits to a shape — a walk, not a coffee, because a walk is easier on both of us and harder to reschedule into nothing. If he says yes we will pick a day, and on that day I will see him, and Chris will become a person from now, or a person from before, and I will know.

I am going to send it before I make lunch. The phone is on the table. The dog is watching. The light is doing its November thing through the window over the sink.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt genuinely proud of something you did — not the achievement kind, the small private kind no one else would have noticed? Describe the moment and what made it land that way.
entries.content:
Two weeks ago, Wednesday, I changed the furnace filter. That is the thing. I want to put it on the page exactly that small, because if I dress it up I will lose what was actually good about it.

The filter had been on the list for a while. Not the bulb-list — the filter is on a different list, the list of things that are nominally on a schedule because the previous owner left a note in the utility closet that said *change every 90 days* in a careful handwriting that I have come to think of as a person speaking to me from across time. I had changed it once, in May, because Jamie had asked me to, and had not done it since, and the date on the little sticker I had written on the frame in May said the next one was due in August, and it was November.

Wednesday afternoon I came in from a walk and instead of going to the kitchen I went down to the basement. I had not decided to do this on the walk. I had not been turning it over. I just came in and went down. I got the new filter from the shelf where there are, I now know, four more of them, because at some point I had bought a multi-pack and forgotten about it, which is its own small piece of evidence about something. I pulled the old one out. It was, I will say, disgusting. Gray-brown in a way that suggested it had been doing real work for a number of months past its useful life. I slid the new one in, oriented the arrow correctly, which I checked twice because I have installed a filter backwards before and felt stupid about it for a week, and I wrote the date on the frame with the Sharpie that lives on the shelf next to the filters. February. I stood there for a second in the basement with the old filter in my hand and the furnace doing its thing and the light from the small window above the workbench making a square on the floor, and I felt — this is the part I want to get right — a small clean thing in the chest that I have not felt in a while.

It was not pride in the achievement sense. The achievement was a four-minute task that I had been three months late on. There was nothing to be proud of in the doing of it. What landed was something more like: I noticed it, and I went down, and I did it, and nobody asked me to, and nobody is going to know. Jamie will not know. The kids will certainly not know. The next person to even register that the filter has been changed will be me, in February, when I look at the date and think *oh, right, time again.* It was a thing I did entirely inside the boundary of myself. The audience was zero. And I did it anyway.

I think what made it land is that it ran counter to the LinkedIn afternoon. The LinkedIn afternoon had been a performance of work for an audience of strangers, ninety minutes, no artifact. The filter was four minutes, no audience, real artifact. The furnace is now drawing air through clean material. There is a measurable, physical difference in the house because of something I did, and the only person who will ever know is the person typing this. Whatever the opposite of the LinkedIn afternoon is, the filter was that. And my body could tell.

I went back upstairs with the old filter and put it in the trash and washed my hands and made a coffee and sat down at the table, and I noticed I felt steadier for about an hour. Not euphoric. Not even particularly cheerful. Just — settled, in the way you are after you have done a small honest thing. The chest-compression I have been writing about, the low-grade hum of unfinished, was quieter for that hour. Then it came back, the way it does, and I went on with the afternoon, and the dog wanted out again, and the day continued.

What I keep turning over is that the furnace filter is, almost exactly, the kind of thing the old me would have considered beneath notice. Not because the old me was above household maintenance — I changed plenty of filters across plenty of years — but because the old me was running on enough external scoreboard that a filter could not register as a positive. The filter was, at best, a non-negative, a thing that did not subtract. To make the scoreboard move you needed a meeting, a deck, a nod. A filter just kept you out of the red.

And here, sitting in the basement at four in the afternoon on a Wednesday in November, with no scoreboard at all, the filter was a positive. It was, in fact, the largest positive of the day. That is the thing I keep coming back to. The scoreboard is gone, and in its absence the small things that used to be invisible have become visible, and some of them are actually carrying weight.
