Not enough weight on their own. I am not going to construct a life out of furnace filters. But weight. Real weight, that registers in the body, that does the thing I used to need a nod for.

I am not going to say this to anyone. That is part of why it lands. The minute I tell Jamie *I changed the filter and felt good about it,* the thing curdles, because I will be looking for her face to do something, and her face will do whatever it does, and I will be back on the scoreboard. The filter has to stay private. The bench is for the bag. The filter is for the furnace. The proud part is for me.

In February I will go down again. I have already, without meaning to, started looking forward to it.

entries.prompt: Who in your life right now do you find yourself softening around — speaking more carefully, choosing your words, leaving things unsaid? Pick a recent moment with them and tell me what you didn't say and what you said instead.
entries.content:
Mom, on the phone again, last Tuesday at 7:15 like clockwork, and this time I tried the cleaner answer I had half-promised myself I would try, and I want to write down what happened because what happened is that I softened in the middle of it and gave her a version that was, again, not quite the thing.

The cleaner version, the one I had been carrying around since the last call, was something like: *I'm okay. I'm taking my time on purpose. I'll tell you when there's news.* Three sentences. I had said them out loud in the car earlier that day, just to hear them, and they had sounded fine. Plain. Adult. The kind of thing a 47-year-old man can say to his mother without it becoming a referendum.

She did the opening — the weather, a thing about the church potluck, an update on her friend Carol's hip — and then she did the slide. "And how are you doing, sweetheart? Anything new with the work?"

And what I said was: "I'm doing fine. Pat and I are still thinking through some directions, and I've been doing some writing on my own. It's been a productive few weeks."

Productive. *Writing on my own.* I did not write the document. I have not opened the document in — at this point I have lost the count and stopped checking, which is itself a form of mercy I am extending to myself. I had circled the LinkedIn profile for ninety minutes. I had updated a furnace filter. The word "productive" was doing the work of three different lies.

What I had been going to say was the cleaner version. What stopped me, mid-breath, was that I could hear, on her end, the small sound she makes when she is sitting in her chair by the lamp, the chair my father used to sit in, and the sound was a sound of attention, of leaning forward, of a 76-year-old woman in a quiet house waiting to hear how her son was. And in the half second before I spoke I felt, very clearly, that the cleaner version — *I'm okay, I'm taking my time on purpose* — would arrive in that lamp-lit room as a kind of door closing. As a son saying, gently but firmly, *please stop asking.* And I could not, with the sound of her chair in my ear, be the person closing that door on her on a Tuesday evening in November.

So I softened. I gave her productive. I gave her writing on my own. I gave her a Pat she could imagine as a colleague rather than a peer in the same fog. I rounded all the edges.

What I did not say was: Mom, I am okay in the way you are asking, but I am not okay in the way you would prefer. I am not assembling a thing. I am sitting with the absence of a thing on purpose, and the sitting is the work, and I cannot describe the sitting in a way that will make your chair feel any less worried. I do not have a noun for you. I may not have a noun for a long time. The cleanest thing I can tell you is that I am not in trouble, and I know that is not what you are checking on, you are checking on whether I have made a mistake, and the honest answer is I do not know yet, and neither do you, and we are going to have to wait this out together with imperfect information, which is harder for you than it is for me because you are an hour away with a phone and a chair.

I did not say any of that. I said *productive.*

She said, "Oh, that's good, sweetheart. I'm glad to hear it." And there was a pause, and in the pause I could hear her deciding whether to ask one more thing or to accept what I had given her and move on, and she moved on. She told me a long story about a neighbor's grandchild's wedding that I half-listened to, and we did our closing, and we said our I love yous, and I hung up.

Jamie was, again, at the table. Not pretending not to listen. Just present. She said, "How is she," and I said, "She's good, the potluck went well," and Jamie said, "And you?" — which she does not usually do, that follow-up — and I looked at her, and I said, "I gave her the soft version again." And Jamie said, "I heard." Just that. *I heard.* And went back to her thing.

I sat down across from her. The dog put her head on my foot. I thought about what it costs me to give Mom the soft version, and I thought about what it would cost her to receive the cleaner one, and I am not sure the math is in favor of clarity. I keep treating the soft version as a failure of honesty, but I think I have to allow that it might also be a kind of care that does not flatter me. She does not need the long form. She needs to know I am still in the world and still her son and still picking up the phone at 7:15. The soft version delivers all three. The cleaner version delivers them too, but with a small chill on it, a *please trust me from a distance* that I am not sure she can yet hear as love.

What I want to be careful about is not letting the softening become permanent. There is a version of this where I soften around her for the next three years and we both come to understand that I will never tell her the actual thing, and the not-telling becomes the relationship, and I do not want that either. I think the move is to let the soft version be the phone version and to save the cleaner version for a time when I am sitting next to her on her couch, with a cup of tea, with no clock on it, and I can give her the long form in pieces, with her face in front of me so I can adjust as I go. The phone is too narrow for the truth. The couch might be wide enough.

Until then I am going to keep saying *productive.* I am going to say it without flinching. I am going to let her have it.

entries.prompt: Last week, when did you find yourself in a room full of people and feel like the only one not quite there? Tell me where you were, what was happening around you, and what you were doing to look like you were keeping up.
entries.content:
Saturday morning, the elementary school gym, the fall fundraiser that Jamie had signed us up for back in September when we were a more optimistic version of ourselves. Long folding tables along three walls with baked goods and raffle items, that particular gym smell of rubber and floor wax and a thousand kid lunches absorbed into the bleachers, fluorescent lights making everyone look slightly worse than they actually look. Maybe sixty parents, plus a handful of teachers, plus Nina somewhere in the gaggle of fifth graders running between the bake sale and the silent auction sheets. Theo had successfully argued his way out of coming. Jamie had been pulled into a conversation about the spring auction with two women on the PTA, which is the conversation she is genuinely good at, and I had been left at the periphery with a paper plate and a brownie I did not want.

What was happening around me was that everyone seemed to know what they were doing. That is the part I want to be honest about. I am sure they didn't, individually, but in the aggregate the room had a competence to it, a kind of low hum of people who had been to fundraisers before and had figured out the protocol. The dad I sort of know from soccer two years ago was running the raffle table with a clipboard and a confidence that suggested he had volunteered for it. Two women I have seen at drop-off were laughing about something near the coffee urn, the kind of laughing that means they have a friendship that exists outside this gym, with calendars and texts and an actual shape. A man in a vest, who I think works at the bank in town, was talking to the principal about something that involved both of them nodding seriously. Everybody had a reason to be where they were standing. I had a brownie.

I drifted. That is what I did, mostly, for about forty minutes. I drifted from the bake sale, where I bought something I did not need from a sixth grader who could not make change, to the silent auction tables, where I pretended to consider bidding on a basket of local honey, to the wall where the kids' art was up, where I stood for a while looking at Nina's piece — a tempera painting of what I was pretty sure was the dog — with my hands in my pockets, performing the role of *engaged parent looking at his child's art.* I think I held that pose for longer than was reasonable. I was using the painting as a small island.

What I was doing to look like I was keeping up was a sequence of small physical things I noticed myself doing only because I was doing them all at once. I was holding the plate at chest height, which is a thing people do when they want to look like they are about to eat the thing on the plate. I was nodding slightly when I caught anyone's eye, the small nod of acknowledgment that says *I see you, we are both adults at the same fundraiser, no further transaction required.* I was checking my phone every few minutes for messages I knew were not there, because the phone is the universal alibi of the man who does not know where to put himself. And I was, when anyone got close enough that a conversation might break out, drifting again, repositioning to a new spot under the guise of looking at a different table.

The conversation I had, when it came, was with a dad whose name I genuinely could not retrieve, though I knew his face from Nina's class two years running. He came up next to me at the silent auction and said something friendly about the honey basket and then asked the question. *So what are you up to these days?* In a gym, on a Saturday, between two folding tables. I gave him the gestural version — coaching, consulting, figuring out the shape — and his face did a smaller, kinder version of the Marcus flicker, and he said something pleasant and moved on toward the raffle. I had been there ninety seconds and I was already exhausted.

What I noticed, standing there afterward with the brownie still untouched, was that the gap between me and the room was not about any of them. None of them were doing anything to me. The bank-vest man was not judging me. The PTA women were not noticing me at all, which is its own thing but not an unkind one. The gap was inside me. I was in a room full of people who had Saturday-morning identities — *I'm here for my kid, I'm here for the school, I'm on the auction committee, I'm the dad with the clipboard* — and I had walked in without one. I was here for my kid too, technically, but the technicality wasn't doing the work the others' Saturday-morning identities were doing for them. They had a peg. I was holding a brownie.

Jamie found me eventually, near the kids' art again, and gave me a look that was equal parts sympathy and amusement. She said, "You okay?" and I said, "I'm fine, just — gymmed out," which is not a phrase but she understood. She said, "Ten more minutes and we can go." And I felt, when she said it, the small grateful collapse of a person who has been holding a posture for forty minutes and has just been told he can put it down soon.

In the car on the way home Nina narrated the entire morning from the back seat, who won what raffle, who had brought what, the social stratigraphy of fifth grade as it had played out at the bake sale, and I let her voice fill the car and did not say much. Jamie put her hand on my leg at a stoplight, briefly, and took it back, which is her shorthand for *I saw, it's okay, we are home in four minutes.*

What I keep coming back to is not the gym. It is that I used to walk into rooms like that and have a peg without thinking about it. The peg used to come with me automatically — *what I do* arrived in the room before I did, and I could spend the actual minutes being a person, a parent, a guy at a fundraiser, because the peg was already in the wall. Without the peg I have to be just a person, in the room, with no advance description, and it turns out I am not yet good at that. I am working on it. The brownie, when I finally ate it in the car, was actually pretty good.

entries.prompt: Describe something you've been carrying a quiet opinion about — a person, a decision someone else made, a way things are being run — that you haven't said out loud. What's the opinion, and what's kept it inside?
entries.content:
The opinion is about Dani, and I have been carrying it for longer than I want to admit.

Mom called Dani in October, after one of our 7:15s, and Dani sent me a text the next morning that said *hey, mom mentioned you're doing some interesting stuff, would love to hear about it whenever.* That was the whole text. *Whenever.* I read it twice and I put the phone down and I have not, in the eight weeks since, written back. Not a thumbs-up. Not anything. The text is still there in the thread, the most recent message, with a small unread-reply quality to it even though I have read it forty times.

The opinion, the one I have not said out loud, is that Dani is not actually asking. Dani is doing a thing that I think Dani has been doing for about fifteen years, which is checking a box that allows Dani to feel like an involved sibling without actually being one. The *whenever* is the giveaway. *Whenever* is a word that means *I have done my part, the ball is in your court, if you do not write back I have still technically reached out and the absence of follow-up is on you.* It is the language of plausible deniability. It is the language of a person who would prefer to be on record as caring than to actually do the thing caring requires, which would be, I don't know, calling. Picking up the phone. Saying *I heard you left the job, I want to know how you are, are you okay.*

That is the opinion. Now I have to be careful, because writing it down has already made me want to soften it, and I want to leave it on the page in its sharp form for a minute before I do the softening, because the softening is part of what has kept it inside.

What has kept it inside is, first, that I am not sure it is fair. Dani lives across the country. Dani has a job and two kids and a life that I do not see most of, and I am inferring an entire emotional posture from one text message, which is a thing I would be the first to call out if someone did it to me. Maybe *whenever* is just how Dani talks. Maybe Dani genuinely meant *whenever, no pressure, on your timeline,* which is, if you read it generously, kind. The generous reading is available. I have just declined to take it.

Second, what has kept it inside is that the opinion is partly about me. If Dani is doing the minimum sibling thing, I have been doing roughly the same minimum sibling thing for years, and the periodic check-ins Jamie and I have always called *warm but careful* with Dani have been, on closer inspection, careful in both directions. I have not called Dani in nine months either. I have not said *hey, this is hard, I would like to tell you about it.* I have, instead, sat in mild grievance about Dani not asking the right way, which is a posture I would find slightly contemptible if I saw it in someone else. The opinion about Dani is also an opinion about me, and saying it out loud — to Jamie, say — would require admitting both halves, and I have not been ready to admit both halves.

Third, and this is the one I am only just getting to: I think I have been holding the opinion as a small reserve of righteousness. As long as Dani is the one not really asking, I am the one who has been not really asked, and there is a small amount of clean grievance available to me in that arrangement. If I write back — if I take the *whenever* at face value and actually tell Dani about the year — then Dani might respond well, and Dani might respond like a real sibling, and the grievance evaporates, and what is left is just me, having not called my sister in years, with no one to blame for the distance but the two of us together. The grievance is load-bearing. It is holding up a story in which the distance is not my fault, or at least not only my fault, and dismantling it would require me to live in a more honest and less flattering account of the relationship.

Mom, by the way, knows. I am sure of this. Mom knows that Dani and I do not really talk, and Mom has been, in her quiet way, trying to bridge it for years — the calls to Dani after the calls to me, the *Dani was asking about you* drops, the gentle steady pressure of a mother who would like her two children to be in each other's lives more than they are. The text in October was, I am almost certain, downstream of one of those calls. Mom had told Dani that she was worried. Dani had felt the small obligation. Dani had sent the *whenever.* And I had received it as evidence of insufficient effort, which was easier than receiving it as Mom's worry traveling around the family looking for somewhere to land.

What has kept it inside, finally, is that I do not know what I want to do with it. Saying the opinion out loud to Jamie would feel like recruiting her to a position, and Jamie is friendly with Dani in the easy uncomplicated way that in-laws sometimes manage to be, and I do not want to taint that. Saying it to Mom is unthinkable. Saying it to Dani directly would require a different kind of conversation than the one Dani offered, and I am not sure I am up for it, and I am not sure Dani has done anything to deserve being on the receiving end of the long version of my year just because Dani phrased a text imperfectly.

So the opinion sits. The text sits. The thread sits. I look at it sometimes when I am scrolling and I scroll past it the way I scrolled past the bulb aisle for two months.

What I am going to do, I think — and I am writing this with less confidence than I wrote the bulb thing, because I am not sure I will — is write back. Not the long version. Not even the medium version. Something like *hey, sorry for the slow reply, things are fine, would be good to talk sometime, I'll call this weekend.* And then call this weekend. Not to deliver an account. Just to put a voice in Dani's ear and let Dani put one in mine, and see whether the *whenever* was a wall or a door. I have been treating it as a wall for eight weeks without checking. The opinion I have been carrying might survive the check. It might not. Either way, the not-checking is starting to cost more than the checking would.

entries.prompt: Think about a place you used to go regularly — a coffee shop, a route you walked, a chair in your own house — that you've stopped going to. Take me there. What was it like when you were still going, and what changed?
entries.content:
The chair in the corner of the bedroom. The one by the window, the one Jamie's mother gave us when she downsized in 2017 and we accepted because you accept furniture from your mother-in-law and figure out what to do with it later. It is upholstered in a fabric that was probably called something like "oatmeal" on the tag, and it has a slight sag in the cushion now from the years I did, in fact, sit in it. There is a small side table next to it with a lamp on it, the lamp Jamie and I bought together at a place that no longer exists, and the table has, since approximately April, become the surface where I put things I do not know where else to put. A book I am partway through. A pair of reading glasses. An envelope from the dentist. The chair, underneath, is doing nothing. It is being a horizontal storage element with a back.

For about six years before that — call it 2018 through early this year — the chair was where I sat for fifteen minutes after I got home from work. Not every night. Most nights. I would come up the stairs, change out of whatever I had been wearing, sit in the chair in the t-shirt and the sweatpants, and do nothing. That was the protocol. I did not bring the phone. I did not bring a book most of the time. I sat. The window faces east, into the back yard, and by the time I sat down it was usually evening light or no light, and I would look out the window or close my eyes or do that half-thing where you are looking but you are not seeing, and I would let the day come off me. Fifteen minutes, give or take. Sometimes Jamie would be downstairs starting dinner and I would hear her through the floor, and the hearing-her-through-the-floor was part of it. The kids might be home, might not. The dog would sometimes come up and put her chin on my knee and then leave when she figured out I was not going to do anything interesting.

I called it, in my own head, the airlock. I never said the word out loud to anyone because it sounded pretentious, but that is what it was. The thing between the outside pressure and the inside pressure where I let the difference equalize. Without it, I would walk in the door from work and be a worse husband and a worse father for about an hour while the work-self slowly shed. With it, I came down the stairs in fifteen minutes as approximately the person the rest of the house deserved. It was, looking back, one of the smarter things I ever did, and I did it without thinking of it as smart. It was just the thing I did when I got home.

What it was like, in the chair, is hard to describe because nothing happened. That was the point. I would feel the day, in a kind of physical way — the meeting that had not gone well, the email I was still composing in my head, the small irritations of the train or the drive — and I would feel it without doing anything about it. It would settle. The chair was the place where the day was allowed to be over. The window helped. The light helped. The fact that nobody came in and asked me anything, because the household had a tacit understanding that the fifteen minutes were mine, helped more than I knew at the time.

What changed is that I stopped going to work. There was no commute, no door, no transition. The day did not have an outside and an inside anymore — the day was the inside, the whole thing, kitchen to office to kitchen to bedroom and back, and there was nothing to equalize from. The first few weeks of being home I went up to the chair anyway, out of habit, and sat in it, and the sitting did not do what it used to do. I was not coming off anything. I was just sitting in a chair in the middle of an undifferentiated afternoon, in a house that was already quiet, and the quiet did not have the same texture as the after-quiet. It was a flat quiet. The chair felt, in a way I could not articulate at the time, embarrassed. Or I was embarrassed, on its behalf, for asking it to do a job that no longer needed doing.

So I stopped. Not as a decision. The way I stop most things now — by drift, by the slow accumulation of not. And the side table, which used to have nothing on it but the lamp, started collecting. The book first, I think. Then the glasses. Then the envelope. Then a second book on top of the first. The chair became the place where books I was not finishing went to wait, and the cushion still has the body-shape in it from six years of evenings, but nothing sits in the shape now.

What I have been thinking about, writing this, is that the airlock was solving a problem I no longer have, which is the gap between two pressures. And in its place I have a different problem, which is that there is no gap anywhere. The day is one long uninflected thing, and I have not built a new airlock for the new shape, because I did not realize I needed one. The walks do some of it. The forty minutes of reading at 6:15 does some of it. But there is no transition between the work-of-the-day and the family-of-the-evening, because the work-of-the-day does not end at a fixed time, it just sort of leaks out into dinner, and I arrive at the table still half inside whatever document I was not writing, and Nina has to ask me twice whether I am listening.

I sat in the chair this morning. I moved the books and the glasses and the envelope onto the bed first, which felt like a small archaeological dig, and then I sat down. The cushion took me back into its shape immediately, which was a strange small kindness. I looked out the window. The maple is bare now. The yard had that late-November look where everything is the color of a paper bag. I did not stay long — maybe four minutes. It was not the airlock. It was something else. A visit. The chair was glad to see me, or I was projecting, which is fine.

I do not know yet what I want the chair to be now. Maybe it can be the place I sit for a few minutes before dinner, instead of after work, since dinner is the closest thing the day still has to a hinge. Maybe it goes back to being a storage surface and that is okay. But I put the books on the dresser when I got up, not back on the table. The lamp is alone again. We will see.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you bought recently — not a big purchase, something small — that you've used differently than you imagined when you got it. What did you picture, and what's actually happened with it?
entries.content:
The notebook. I bought a notebook in early November at the stationery place two towns over, the small one tucked between the bakery and the dry cleaner, where the owner is always reading something behind the counter and barely looks up when you come in. It was a Wednesday. I had driven over for a different reason — I think to pick up a prescription Jamie had asked me to grab — and I had walked past the stationery place and walked back to it and gone in without quite deciding to.

What I picked out was a hardcover, A5, dotted pages, navy. Twenty-eight dollars, which is more than I would have spent on a notebook a year ago and is exactly the kind of small purchase the eleven-p.m. spreadsheet voice would normally have something to say about. The voice did not pipe up. I think the voice understood, in the way the voice sometimes does, that this was a purchase it was not going to win an argument against. I paid in cash because the owner has a sign about card minimums and I did not want to be the guy putting a notebook on a card.

What I pictured, walking out, was almost embarrassingly specific. I pictured myself at the kitchen table, early morning, with the coffee, and the notebook open, and a pen — I would buy a pen too, I had decided in the car, a real one, not a Bic — and I would be writing in it. Not journaling. I have this document for that, and I have, somewhat to my surprise, kept up with it. The notebook was going to be for the work. The thinking-work. The half-drafted document I have not opened in — I checked, briefly, and then stopped checking — was going to migrate, somehow, into the notebook. By hand. The theory, which I had been developing on walks for about three weeks, was that the document was sterile because it was on a screen, and that the screen was the problem, and that if I moved the thinking onto paper the thinking would unstick. The notebook was going to be the unsticking.

I had a whole picture. I had the navy cover lying flat next to the coffee cup. I had my handwriting, which used to be decent and has gone to hell from disuse, gradually recovering its shape over the first ten pages. I had the satisfaction, at the end of a morning, of having filled a side, the small physical evidence of having thought. I had, if I am being honest, a little fantasy of someone — Jamie, maybe, or future-me — picking the notebook up in a year and finding it full, and the full-ness being the proof that something had happened in this stretch.

What has actually happened with the notebook is that I have used it for grocery lists.

Not exclusively. But mostly. The first list went in on the Saturday after I bought it, because Jamie asked me what we needed and I happened to have the notebook on the counter and a pen in my hand, and I wrote *eggs, bread, the cereal Nina likes, dog food, that thing with the orange label* on the first page, and I tore the page out neatly along the perforation — there is a perforation, it is that kind of notebook — and took it to the store. I came back. I made another list a few days later. Then there was a list of things to bring to Theo's school for the conference night, then a list of questions I wanted to ask the vet, then a list of things to pick up at the hardware store, which included, among other things, a second furnace filter even though I have four on the shelf, because I had forgotten about the four on the shelf when I made the list.

The notebook is now about a fifth gone. None of what is in it is the work. It is a household ledger. It is the running operational log of a man who is, apparently, the household's logistics function now, in a way I had not fully named to myself until I saw it in handwriting. *Pick up Nina at 4. Call about the gutters. Mom's prescription.* The notebook, the navy notebook with the dotted pages and the perforation, that I bought to hold the shape of my emerging professional thinking, is full of *milk.*

The first few times I noticed this I felt something close to disappointment. Not quite shame, but in that family. I had bought a tool for one purpose and was using it for another, and the other was smaller, more domestic, less flattering than the picture. The notebook was supposed to be evidence of the inner life and was instead evidence of errands. I almost, on two occasions, bought a second notebook — a different color, a different size, *that* one would be for the work, and *this* one could keep being for the lists — and I caught myself, both times, before I left the house. Buying a second notebook to protect the first notebook from its actual use is the kind of move I am trying to do less of.

What I have come around to, slowly, is that the notebook is doing something I did not know I needed. The lists used to live on the back of envelopes, on the corner of the school flyer, on whatever piece of paper was nearest the moment Jamie said *can you* and then in fragments in my head, and then the fragments would lose pieces, and I would come home from the store without the cereal Nina likes. Since the notebook, the lists are in one place, and the things on the lists are getting bought, and the household is, in a small measurable way, running better. Jamie has not commented on this, which is how I know it is working. The things that work in this house are the things she does not have to mention.

The work is still not happening on paper. The document is still not open. The notebook has not unstuck anything, because, I think, nothing was going to be unstuck by a notebook. The stuckness is not a stationery problem. I had assigned the notebook a job it could not do, and the notebook, sensibly, has taken a different job, the one actually available, and is doing it well.

I am leaving it on the counter now, not on the table where I had pictured it. The counter is where the lists happen. The pen — I did buy the pen, a fine-point rollerball, fourteen dollars — lives next to it. When I sit down with the morning coffee I do not bring the notebook. I bring this. The two surfaces have sorted themselves.

Maybe that is fine. The picture I had was nicer, but the picture I had was also, I notice, a picture in which the notebook was for the version of me I have been trying to assemble, and what the notebook is actually for is the version of me that already exists, the one that buys the cereal. That version is doing its job. I should let it have a tool.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a moment in the last week where you caught yourself being kinder to someone else than you'd be to yourself in the same situation. What did you say to them, and what would the version aimed at you have sounded like?
entries.content:
Wednesday afternoon Pat called me, which was unusual — Pat texts, mostly, and we had agreed not to do anything for a month and the month is almost up but not quite — and the call was Pat saying, in a voice I did not recognize at first, that the workshop idea Pat had been quietly continuing to think about even though we had said we wouldn't, and had started outlining solo over the weekend, was not coming together. Pat had spent four days on it. Pat had, Pat said, "made a draft and looked at the draft and the draft was bad and then made another draft and the other draft was also bad," and Pat was calling me, I gathered, partly to say it out loud to someone who would understand the specific flavor of the badness and partly because Pat sounded a little untethered and needed a voice in the ear.

I said the things I said. I said *that's the work, that is literally what the work looks like at this stage.* I said *four days is nothing, you cannot judge a thing in four days, the four days are part of how you find out what the thing is.* I said *the draft being bad is information, it is not a verdict, you are confusing the two things.* I said *you are allowed to make a bad draft, that is what drafts are for, the badness is the toll you pay for the next version.* I said *be kind to yourself, this is hard.*

I said *be kind to yourself.* In those words. I do not think I have ever said that phrase out loud to another human being before. It came out of my mouth as if it had been sitting in a drawer, fully formed, waiting. Pat made a small sound that I think was a laugh and said, "thanks, I needed that," and we talked for another twenty minutes about specifics — what was in the draft, what wasn't, what Pat thought it was supposed to do — and by the end of the call Pat sounded like Pat again, and we hung up, and I sat at the kitchen table with the phone in my hand for a minute looking at the wall.

What I noticed, sitting there, was that I had spent twenty minutes giving Pat a version of grace I have not given myself once in nine months.

Because the document. The half-drafted document. The one I have not opened in — and I will say it, I checked, the count is now thirty-four days. I have stopped circling that number and started quietly bracing against it. And the way I have been talking to myself about the document, the actual interior monologue when I see the icon and click past it, is not anything close to *the badness is information.* It is closer to *what is wrong with you. You have had nine months. You have had cleared mornings and cleared afternoons and a spouse who is paying half the rent on this fog and you cannot open a file.* It is closer to *every day you do not work on it is a day of evidence about who you actually are when no one is watching, and the evidence is accumulating.* It is closer to a small mean voice in a slightly older register saying *a real person would have a draft by now.*

That is the version aimed at me. If I had picked up the phone Wednesday and Pat had been me, the version I would have delivered, in the same voice, would have been: *what have you been doing all this time. You are going to run out of runway. Pat is making drafts. Pat is at least making drafts. You are not even bad at the work, you are not at the work.* The dad in the cereal aisle voice, the one who could tell what a man did by how he answered the question at a barbecue, dressed up as productivity advice.

I do not know how to account for this gap honestly. I do not think it is that I am a generous person to others and a hard one to myself, though that is the flattering frame and I would be glad to take it. I think what is closer to true is that I can see Pat's situation from the outside, which means I can see how unreasonable the harshness would be if I directed it at Pat, and I cannot see my own situation from the outside, so the harshness has nothing to push against. From inside my own head, *thirty-four days* is damning. From inside Pat's head, four days of bad drafts is, presumably, also damning. The damnation is a feature of the inside view. It is what the inside view does.

What I would like to do — and I am writing this carefully, because I do not trust myself to follow through and I have been trying to make fewer notebook-promises in this document — is repeat to myself, the next time I see the icon and click past it, the actual sentence I said to Pat. *Be kind to yourself. This is hard.* Not as a mantra. Just as a piece of evidence I have produced, in my own voice, on the record, that I do believe this about another adult in roughly my situation, and that the believing is not contingent on Pat being a different kind of person than I am. Pat and I are, by Pat's own account on the phone, doing the same thing. The kindness should travel both ways or it isn't kindness, it's preference.

I am not sure it will travel. The mean voice has tenure. It has been there my whole working life, and it has, I should say, gotten me places, which is part of why I am reluctant to fire it — there is a story in which the mean voice is what made me competent, and firing it is what would make me soft. I am suspicious of that story tonight but I am not done with it.

What I told Pat, I will at least try not to un-tell. If Pat calls back next week and I have spent the week being meaner to myself than I was to Pat on Wednesday, the meanness is going to be harder to hold with a straight face, because I will hear my own voice from the call in it, and the voice will know.

entries.prompt: Where in the last week did you feel a flash of envy — small, maybe ugly, the kind you'd rather not admit to? Tell me who or what set it off, where you were when it hit, and what you did with it in the moment.
entries.content:
The flash came Sunday evening, on the couch, with the laptop on my legs and the dog asleep against my hip and the house otherwise quiet because Jamie had taken the kids to her sister's for dinner and I had begged off, citing nothing in particular. I was not even doing anything. I was on the news site I default to when I do not know what I want to do, scrolling, and the algorithm, which knows me better than it should, served up a piece about somebody from my old industry who had left around the same time I did — not someone I knew, someone two rungs above me in a place I never worked — and had, the piece reported, just announced a new venture with a name and a website and a small round of funding from people whose names were also in the piece. There was a photograph. The man was standing in front of a window in a nice shirt looking thoughtful in the way people in those photographs are always looking thoughtful, like they have just been asked a question and are about to answer it well.

The flash hit before I had finished the first paragraph. Hot in the chest, quick in the throat, the specific feeling of being passed on the highway by a car you had not noticed in your mirror. I want to be precise about it because the precision is the only thing that keeps me honest. It was not admiration. Admiration is cooler, more distant, has a kind of room in it. This was tighter. It was *that should be me,* in those words, almost subvocalized, and underneath *that should be me* a second sentence that was *and you know why it isn't.*

I read the rest of the piece. I read it twice. I clicked through to the website, which was clean and minimal in the way these websites always are, with a manifesto-ish paragraph on the landing page that I read three times trying to figure out what the actual thing was. I am not sure I figured it out. There was a noun in the manifesto and the noun was doing a lot of work and I could not tell, by the end, whether the noun referred to a real product or to a posture, and I noticed, when I noticed I could not tell, a small mean satisfaction that I tried to enjoy and could not, because the satisfaction did not undo the flash, it only stacked on top of it like a second feeling lying about the first.

What I did with it, in the moment, was the bad version. I am going to write the bad version because lying about it on the page would defeat the page. I clicked through to the man's profile on the platform I had spent ninety minutes editing my own headline on three weeks ago. I read his headline. I read his summary. I scrolled his recent posts. There were three of them, all in the last month, each with the small heart-and-clap counts in the hundreds. I read the comments. I recognized two names in the comments from my old industry, people who had not, I noticed with a specificity I am embarrassed by, ever commented on anything I had posted, because I had never posted anything. I sat with the laptop hot on my legs and the dog snoring and I performed, for an audience of zero, a full forensic comparison between this man's nine months and my nine months, and his came out ahead on every visible axis, because the visible axes are the ones the platform is built to display, and my axes — the filter, the laughing at the dog, the bench is for the bag — do not have counters next to them.

I did this for, I would estimate, thirty-five minutes. By the end I felt a particular kind of tired, the tired of having lost a small fight with myself in slow motion. I closed the laptop. The dog opened one eye and closed it. The house did its evening sounds, the furnace coming on, which I noticed because of the filter, which I refused to take comfort from in that moment because I was committed to the bad mood and a clean filter was not going to be allowed to lift it.

What I want to be honest about, more than the envy itself, is the second sentence. *And you know why it isn't.* That sentence is the one I am still turning over. Because the sentence implied an answer it did not name, and the answer it did not name was something like: because you are not building anything. Because you are reading and walking and updating a profile you do not publish. Because the man in the nice shirt has spent his nine months doing the visible thing, and you have spent yours in a chair and a yard and a basement, and the visible thing is what the world rewards and you knew that going in and you chose this anyway and now you would like to be rewarded for the invisible thing too, which is greedy.

That is the sentence under the sentence. I do not entirely buy it. I notice that on Wednesday I told Pat that drafts being bad was information and not a verdict, and the man in the nice shirt has, by the available evidence, made a draft I cannot evaluate from a website and a platform, and I am happy to deliver a verdict on his nine months from my couch on the basis of three posts and a manifesto. I am being to him what I would not be to Pat, and what I am refusing to be to myself, which means I am being three different people in three different rooms and the inconsistency is the tell.

I went to bed before Jamie got home. Lying there, I thought: the envy is real, and the envy is data, and the data is — I am not as far along as I had quietly hoped I would be by now, and seeing a stranger be further along has poked the hope, and the hope is in worse shape than I had been admitting. That is the thing under the envy. Not *I want what he has,* exactly. More like *I want to be further along than I am,* and the man in the nice shirt was the occasion, not the cause.

I did not look at the website again Monday morning. That is what I did with it, in the end, or what I am doing with it. I made coffee. The dog came down. The kettle did its thing. The man in the nice shirt is presumably also having his Monday morning, somewhere, and I hope, in the alumni way, that it is going well for him. I mostly mean it.

entries.prompt: When did you last lie awake when you wanted to be asleep? Take me into that stretch — what time it was, what the room felt like, and where your mind kept going when you tried to steer it somewhere else.
entries.content:
Tuesday into Wednesday, somewhere between two and four, though I did not look at the clock more than once because I have learned that looking at the clock is a way of starting a small argument with the rest of the night you cannot win. The room was cold in the particular way it gets when the heat has cycled off and the wind is doing something against the north side of the house — not a noise, exactly, more a pressure, the windows feeling slightly busier than usual. Jamie was asleep on her side facing away, breathing in the rhythm she has, the dog was on the rug at the foot of the bed making the small wet sound she makes in her own dreams, and I was on my back with my eyes open looking at the ceiling fan, which we do not run in November but which is still up there, blades visible in the half-light from the hallway nightlight Nina insisted on at age six and we have somehow never taken down.

What got me up there in the first place — not up out of bed, just up into the awake — was the runway tab. I had updated it Sunday and had not really looked at the number since, and somewhere around one-thirty my brain produced it unprompted, the way it produces phone numbers, and the number was thirteen. Thirteen months. It had been fourteen the time before. The drop from fourteen to thirteen is, in dollar terms, exactly what we spend in a month, which is the point of the tab, and yet seeing the integer click down by one felt like something more than arithmetic. It felt, lying there, like the spreadsheet was a clock with a different unit, and I had just heard it tick.

I tried the things I try. I tried the breathing, the four-in-seven-hold-eight-out, which I read about somewhere and which works about a third of the time and did not work this time. I tried the trick where you name what you can hear in the room — the furnace, the wind, Jamie, the dog, the small tick from the radiator pipe in the corner — and that bought me about ninety seconds before the runway tab came back. I tried thinking about the dinner from Friday, the laughing, the dog hitting her head, which I had been using like a small warm coin in my pocket all week, and the coin worked for a minute and then went cool the way coins do.

Where my mind kept going, when I tried to steer it somewhere else, was a kind of accountancy I am embarrassed by. Not the spreadsheet — I am at peace with the spreadsheet, sort of, in daylight. The other accountancy. The one where I was tallying, in the dark, what I had actually produced in the last thirty days. Not produced as in shipped. Produced as in done, in any sense, that I could point to. I had the filter. I had the bulb. I had the notebook full of grocery lists. I had the call with Pat where I had said *be kind to yourself.* I had not opened the document. The document was the loud item on the list and it stayed loud no matter what I put next to it. *Thirty-four days,* my brain said, in the older parental register, and then because brains are stupid at two in the morning, *no, thirty-six now, you stopped counting on a Monday and it is Wednesday.* The two extra days, because they had arrived without my permission, felt heavier than the original thirty-four. I had not earned them. They had just shown up.

Then the mind went to Dani. Then it went to the text I still had not sent. Then it went to Mom, and the word *productive,* and the chair my father used to sit in, which somewhere between two-thirty and three became, against my will, the chair I had been picturing him in, and from him to the line about the barbecue, and from the barbecue to the man in the nice shirt in front of the window, who at two-thirty in the morning was no longer a stranger I was trying to be alumni-generous toward but a kind of reproach with a haircut. I could not summon the daytime version of myself who had told Pat the badness was information. The daytime version was asleep, evidently, somewhere I could not get to. The night version had different staffing.

What I did, eventually, was give up on sleep without admitting I was giving up. I got out of bed slowly so as not to wake Jamie, went downstairs in socks, did not turn on the kitchen light, and stood in the dark by the sink with a glass of water for what was probably ten minutes. The dog had followed me down, because the dog, like me, considers nighttime movement an event. The house at three in the morning is a different house. The refrigerator has a louder hum than the daytime version of itself. The window over the sink had a little frost-suggestion in the corner. I drank the water. I did not, and want to note this, look at my phone. I did not check email. I did not open the news site. That is the only piece of the night I would say I did well.

Then I did something I had not planned to do. I went down to the basement. Not to do anything. Just to stand for a minute in the place I had felt the small clean thing two weeks before, by the furnace, in the square of light from the workbench window — except there was no light, of course, it was three in the morning, and the basement was just the basement, dark, smelling faintly of the dryer. I stood there in my socks on the cold floor and the furnace was running, doing the quiet competent thing it does, drawing air through the filter I had put in, and I thought — this is the part I want to keep — *something in this house is working.* That sentence, exactly. Something in this house is working, and you put it there, and it does not need you to keep showing up to admire it, it just runs.

I went back upstairs. I got into bed. Jamie shifted, did not wake. I do not know what time I finally went down. I know it was before the sky started, because when the alarm went at six-fifteen the room was still dark, and I felt the specific used-up feeling of a night spent mostly defending against itself, but I also felt, faintly, the basement. Which is not nothing. Which is, I am going to decide for the purposes of getting through Wednesday, enough.

entries.prompt: There's a thing you do — or don't do — when you're alone in the house and nobody's expecting anything from you for the next hour. Tell me about the last stretch like that. What did you actually do with it, and what does that say that a busier hour wouldn't?
entries.content:
Yesterday, two-fifteen to about three-thirty, Jamie at the office for an in-person thing she does once a month, Theo at a friend's, Nina at the dance class on Wednesdays that runs an hour and ten minutes door to door. The dog had been out twice already and was committed to the rug. The house was, as the prompt has it, expecting nothing from me. I had seventy-five minutes of clean unallocated time in a daylit kitchen with a coffee that was still warm.

What I actually did, in order, was: stood at the sink for a minute looking at the back yard. Refilled the coffee. Sat down at the table. Opened the laptop. Did not open the document. Opened the news site. Read two pieces I will not be able to summarize. Closed the news site. Got up. Walked to the front room. Stood there. Walked back. Sat down. Picked up the book I have been almost finished with for a week and read four pages. Put the book down. Got up again. Went to the window over the sink. Watched a squirrel do something stupid on the fence for a stretch I am going to estimate, generously, at six minutes. Came back to the table. Did not open the document. Looked at the notebook on the counter, with its grocery lists, and decided not to make a list because there was nothing to put on it. Sat down. Refilled the coffee a second time, which was a mistake, because the second refill was already cold by the time I poured it. Drank it anyway. Read the four pages again because I had not absorbed them the first time. Heard a car door somewhere down the street and looked up before realizing it was not Jamie, who would not be home for hours. Sat back. The dog rolled onto her other side. The light moved on the floor by maybe an inch.

That is the report. Seventy-five minutes, no artifact, no decision, no movement on anything I would have, in advance, said I wanted to use the time for. If you had asked me at two-fifteen what I would do with seventy-five minutes alone in the house, I would have said *probably the document,* and I would have half-meant it, the way you half-mean things you have been not doing for thirty-six days. I did not do the document. I also did not, and I want to note this, do the LinkedIn move, the dressed-up not-doing. I did not perform. I just sat. And got up. And sat. And looked at a squirrel.

What I want to figure out, writing this, is whether what I did counts as rest or as something worse. Because there is a version where I am being too generous with myself — where the seventy-five minutes were a small failure of will dressed up as a contemplative interlude, and the right name for it is *I drifted again,* and the drift is the pattern I have been watching get larger, from the bulb to the document to the text to Dani. The squirrel and the four pages and the two refills are, on that reading, the fine-grained detail of an avoidance, and noticing the fine-grained detail does not redeem the avoidance, it just makes it prettier on the page.

There is another version. The other version is that the busy hour, the hour with someone expecting something from me, would have produced an artifact — a school form filled out, a load of laundry moved, a list executed — and the artifact would have been, in the way I have been learning to recognize, slightly false comfort. Performed working. The unbusy hour produced no artifact, and the absence of the artifact is uncomfortable in a way the artifact would have anaesthetized, and the discomfort might be the actual content. The discomfort might be what the hour was for. I sat with not-knowing-what-to-do-with-myself for seventy-five minutes, and that is, on its face, exactly the thing I left the old life to be able to sit with, and now that I can sit with it I would like to report that it was profound and it was not. It was just sitting. With a squirrel.

What I think a busier hour would not have shown me is how small the gap is, when nothing is expected, between the version of me who is at peace with this stretch and the version who is not. In the busy hour the two versions are merged into a person doing things, and the doing covers the seam. In the empty hour the seam is right there. I noticed yesterday, around two-forty, that I was not bored, exactly, and I was not anxious, exactly, and I was not at peace, exactly — I was in some weather between them that does not have a clean name, and the weather kept changing every few minutes without warning. One stretch of looking at the squirrel was almost contented. The next stretch, three minutes later, looking at the same squirrel, had a small dread in it about the runway tab. Same window. Same squirrel. Different inside.

That is what the empty hour says that the busy one does not. It says the work-of-the-day, when there is no work-of-the-day, becomes the management of those small flickers between weathers, and that I am not yet very good at it, but I am, I think, slightly better than I was in March, when an hour like yesterday's would have ended with me on the couch refreshing something. Yesterday I did not refresh anything. I closed the news tab after two pieces. I picked up the book. I let the squirrel be the event. That is, by the standards I am quietly building for myself, a passing grade.

Nina came home at three-thirty-five with a story about a girl who had cried during the across-the-floor exercise, and the seventy-five minutes ended the way these things end — abruptly, with someone coming in the door, the dog going to greet her, the kitchen filling with somebody else's afternoon. I closed the laptop without anything new in it. The coffee cup went in the sink. I had not done the document. I had also not done the thing I do instead of the document. Maybe that is the small win. Maybe the empty hour is where I learn to do neither, and from neither, eventually, something else.

entries.prompt: When you imagine telling someone — a stranger on a plane, say — what you do for a living right now, what's the answer that comes out of your mouth, and what's the answer you'd give if you trusted them with the real one?
entries.content:
The stranger-on-a-plane is not hypothetical this week, because I had something close to it on Saturday at a wedding-shower brunch thing for one of Jamie's cousins, where I was seated next to a man I had never met named Alan, whose wife was Jamie's cousin's college roommate, and who was at this event the same way I was, which is by marriage and without a clear social mandate. Alan was pleasant. Alan was a structural engineer of some kind, which he established within the first two minutes in a way that was not bragging, just informational, the way people lay down the first piece of a conversational floor. Then he asked me. He asked it in the friendliest possible register, with a small smile, while we were both looking at the buffet. *And what about you, what do you do?*

What came out of my mouth was: "I'm doing some independent advisory work right now, kind of in transition from a longer career in services. Bit of coaching, bit of consulting." Three sentences. Spoken without visible effort. Alan nodded and said, "That's interesting, what kinds of clients?" and I said, "Mostly people in similar industries thinking about next steps," which is a sentence so close to true that it would survive a polygraph and so close to nothing that it could mean anything, and Alan, sensing correctly that the topic had been answered to the level the host intended, moved us cleanly to whether I had tried the egg thing on the buffet, which I had not.

That is the answer that comes out. I want to look at it for a second because I keep being surprised by how smooth it has gotten. Nine months ago I could not say a sentence about my situation without it coming out sideways. Now I have a small set of phrases — *independent advisory work, in transition, similar industries, next steps* — that go together like a cheap suit, off the rack, fits-everyone, no alterations. *Bit of coaching, bit of consulting* in particular is doing tremendous work. The *bit of* is the trick. It signals modesty and breadth simultaneously and forecloses the follow-up, because *bit of* is not a thing anyone is going to ask you to elaborate on. You do not ask a man at a brunch to specify the bit. The *bit of* is a closed door with a friendly knocker on it.

The answer I would have given Alan if I trusted him with the real one — and Alan, I want to say, seemed perfectly trustworthy, that was not the obstacle, the obstacle was that we had thirty seconds of buffet adjacency and the real answer does not fit in thirty seconds — would have been something like this:

I left the long career nine months ago because being good at it had stopped feeling like the same thing as wanting to do it, and I did not have a next thing lined up, which I know is the part of the sentence that does most of the work. I have some money. Not a lot, but enough that we can run this experiment for another stretch if we are careful, and we are careful people. I am poking at some adjacent work with another person, and we have, on purpose, agreed not to do anything for a month, and the month is almost up. I am doing some writing, by which I mean I have a half-drafted document I have not opened in something like thirty-eight days, and I am, separately and more reliably, writing privately about what this stretch is actually like, which is the only piece of the work I trust right now. I read a lot. I take walks. My kids are eleven and fourteen and I am present for them in a way I was not when they were smaller, which is a gift that is also, I am learning, a small grief. I do not yet know whether what I am doing is the early shape of a thing or just a stretch I am in, and I have a working hypothesis that the answer will not arrive as a noun, which is a sentence that sounds either spiritual or evasive and I am neither of those things by temperament. I am a person who used to make slide decks. I am at this brunch holding a plate. That is what I do.

I have written some version of that paragraph in this document several times over and I keep being surprised that it is still the answer. It has not gotten shorter or cleaner, only more accurate. The accuracy is not what makes it sayable to a stranger. The accuracy is what makes it sayable to me.

What I notice, comparing the two — the *bit of coaching, bit of consulting* and the long version — is that the gap between them is not a gap of honesty exactly. It is a gap of audience. Alan did not need the long version. Alan needed a category that would let him put me down on the brunch seating chart of his mind and move on to whether the egg thing was any good. Giving Alan the long version would have been a kind of category violation — would have asked Alan to be Pat, or Jamie, or this document, when Alan had only signed up to be Alan. That would not have been honesty. It would have been an imposition wearing honesty as a costume. I have done that move, in earlier versions of this stretch, with people who did not deserve to be the recipients of my unfinished thinking, and what I learned is that nobody is helped by it, including me. The unfinished thinking does not get finished by being said to a structural engineer at a buffet. It just gets aired in a room that cannot hold it, and then I have to walk back to the table feeling like I have left something on the carpet.

The version I would give if I trusted Alan with the real one is, I am realizing, maybe not a version I would give Alan at all. It is a version I would give a person who had asked twice, who had stayed at the buffet, who had said something like *no, but really.* Almost nobody does that. Alan certainly did not, and was right not to. The trustworthy person is not the one who can hear the long version. The trustworthy person is the one who would think to ask for it, and they are rare, and most of them I already know.

So what I gave Alan was, on inspection, the right answer for the conversation Alan and I were actually in. *Bit of coaching, bit of consulting.* And what I gave myself, walking back to the table with a plate, was the long version, again, in my head, for the audience of one who has been listening this whole time and is, slowly, learning to take it on faith.

entries.prompt: Think about a recent moment with one of the kids — Theo or Nina — where they said or did something that stuck with you afterward. Take me into the moment, and tell me what you've been turning over about it since.
entries.content:
Theo, Sunday afternoon, in the car, the two of us driving back from the sporting goods place where I had taken him to get cleats because the old ones had finally given up in a way that even he could not deny. He had been quiet on the drive over, which is his default, and quiet in the store, where he had picked the cleats with a brisk efficiency that suggested he had already done the research on his phone and was simply executing. We were maybe ten minutes from home, on the road that goes past the reservoir, the late-afternoon light doing the November thing where everything looks slightly more honest than it is, and out of nowhere, without preamble, he said: "Are you okay, though? Like with the not-working thing."

I want to get the sentence right because I have been replaying it. *Are you okay, though.* The *though* is what I keep coming back to. The *though* implied a conversation he had been having on his own that I had not been part of, in which there was a first half — *I can see things are fine on the surface* — and the *though* was the pivot to the second half, the actual question, which had been waiting in him for who knows how long. He did not look at me when he said it. He was looking out the passenger window at the reservoir, which had a couple of geese on it.

I said something stupid first. I said, "Yeah, buddy, I'm okay. Why do you ask?" Which is the parental dodge, the *why do you ask* that sends the question back across the net so the parent can see the swing again before deciding how to play it. He shrugged, the small fourteen-year-old shrug that means *I am not going to do the work of explaining my question to you, you have to come to me,* and we drove for another quarter mile in silence, and I realized I was about to lose the moment if I did not actually answer.

So I tried. I said, "It's been a weird year. I'm figuring some things out. It's slower than I thought it would be." I said, "Mom and I are fine, the money is fine, I want you to know that part." I said, "I don't have a clean answer for what I'm doing yet, and I think I won't for a while, and that's harder for me than it probably looks." I said, "But yeah. I'm okay." Five sentences, give or take, and they came out without much rehearsal, which surprised me, because I have been rehearsing some version of that paragraph for nine months in case the wrong adult asked, and the right kid asked and most of the rehearsal was not needed.

He nodded. Just nodded. Did not turn from the window. After a beat he said, "Okay. I just wondered." And then, after another beat, with the same small flatness: "You seem better than in the summer."

*Better than in the summer.* I have been turning that one over more than the question itself. Because I had not known he was grading the summer. I had not known the summer was being graded. I had thought, in the way parents of teenagers are wrong about most things, that Theo had been sealed inside his own fourteen-year-old weather since around June, occupied with whatever Theo is occupied with, and that the weather inside the rest of the house had been mine and Jamie's to manage out of his sightline. He had been watching. He had a baseline. He had a comparison. He had decided, on his own, that I was *better than in the summer,* which means in the summer I was worse, and he had registered the worse, and had been carrying it.

I asked him, carefully, what he meant. He did the shrug again. He said, "You were just kind of — I don't know. You'd be in the kitchen but not really. Like you were doing something in your head." And then, because he is fourteen and the conversation was getting close to the thing fourteen-year-olds protect themselves from, he said, "Anyway, the cleats are good. Thanks for getting them," and I knew the window had closed and that I should not pry it back open.

We drove the rest of the way home and I asked him about the spring season and he told me about the coach in a way that was longer than I expected, and we pulled into the driveway and he took the bag with the cleats and went inside, and I sat in the car for a minute before following him in.

What I have been turning over since is that I had a story about what I was protecting them from. The story was that the disorientation of this year was a thing I was carrying separately, in private, in the document, in this document, on walks, at one in the morning, and that the household-facing version of me was a fully landed person who was making dinner and asking about homework and being present in the new way I had been telling myself I was being present. *More present to give,* I wrote, way back. I had given myself credit for that. And Theo, without knowing the language of any of it, had clocked that the present-version was not always present, that there were stretches in the summer when I was, as he put it, *doing something in my head* in the kitchen, and the kitchen-presence had been, on those days, a costume. He had not bought the costume. He had been polite about the costume. But he had known.

Which means a couple of things. It means the laughing at dinner with the dog, the four of us, was even more of a real event than I had registered, because Theo had been paying closer attention to the temperature in this house than I had given him credit for, and his laughter was not a baseline being met but a baseline being exceeded. It means the *I know* in his bedroom after the backpack night was also more loaded than I let it be, because *I know* was not just *I know it wasn't about the bag,* it was *I know there is a thing going on with you, and I have known for a while, and I am being careful with you about it.* My fourteen-year-old has been being careful with me. I have been thinking of myself as the parent doing the careful work and he has, quietly, been doing some of his own.

And it means that the *better than in the summer* is the only piece of feedback on this stretch that I trust completely, because Theo has nothing to gain by issuing it. Jamie's *good* about going to talk to him is shaped by love and by living next to me. Pat's read on me is shaped by being in the same fog. Mom's is shaped by worry. Theo's *better* is shaped by nothing — he is fourteen, he is not running a project on me, he was looking at geese — and it lands as the cleanest sentence anyone has said about my year.

I am not going to make it into a thing with him. I am not going to bring it up at dinner or write him a long text or any of the moves I would have made at thirty. I am going to let the conversation in the car be the conversation in the car. The cleats are by the back door. He wore them out in the yard yesterday on the half-frozen grass to break them in, and from the kitchen window I watched him do the small testing turns kids do with new shoes, and I thought: he asked, and I answered, and he is fourteen, and he is here, and that is the report.

entries.prompt: Is there a compliment or piece of praise someone gave you recently that didn't quite land — that you nodded at but didn't really take in? Tell me who said it, what they said, and what was going on inside you while you were accepting it.
entries.content:
The compliment came from Jamie's sister Beth at Thanksgiving, which we hosted, which was its own decision and its own small triumph that I am still mildly suspicious of. She had cornered me in the kitchen between the turkey coming out and the side dishes going in, with that particular Beth energy of someone who has had one and a half glasses of wine and is now in her sincere phase, and she said: "I have to say, you seem really *good*. Like — settled. Whatever you're doing, it's working." She put her hand on my arm when she said *settled,* briefly, and then went to find the cranberry thing, and I said "thanks" to her back.

What was going on inside me while I was accepting it was a small, quick, three-part move I have started to recognize. The first part was a flicker of pleasure, real, warm, the kind of warmth a compliment from someone who is not obligated to give one produces in the chest. The second part, arriving maybe a half second behind, was a small flat refusal — *no I don't, you are wrong, you are seeing the kitchen and not the spreadsheet.* The third part was the move I made to manage the gap between the first two, which was to nod and say thanks and not actually take it in, the way you accept a package at the door without opening it because you suspect the contents will require a decision.

The package is still in the hall, as it were. I am writing about it because I noticed, this morning, that I have been replaying Beth's sentence for three days, which is not what you do with compliments you have received. It is what you do with compliments you have refused.

What I think I refused, specifically, is *settled.* The other words I could have taken. *Good* is generic enough to slide off. *Working* is about a process, not me, and I am happy to credit a process. *Settled* was the one with the hook. Because *settled* is a verdict on the inside, and the inside is the thing I have spent nine months telling myself nobody else can see, and the only person whose verdict on the inside I have been willing to accept lately is Theo's *better than in the summer.* Beth is not Theo. Beth has not been here. Beth was making, by my private accounting, a generous guess from a small sample — one Thanksgiving, a kitchen, a husband stirring something — and the guess happened to land on a word I had not given her permission to use.

Why didn't I let it in. Sitting here this morning I think the actual reason is that taking *settled* in the front door would mean acknowledging that someone outside the house can see a version of me that I have been crediting only to myself, on long walks, in basements with furnaces. If Beth can see it from a kitchen on a Thursday in November, then it is not a private thing I am carefully maintaining against the wind. It is, at least sometimes, visible. And if it is visible, then it is also vulnerable, in the way visible things are — it can be observed not being there next time, it can be fact-checked, it can be revoked. The private version has the safety of being unfalsifiable. The visible version comes with stakes.

There is also a smaller, less flattering reason, which is that I have been quietly grading my own progress on a curve that requires the world to underestimate me. The story I have half-told myself is that I am doing real work in here that nobody can see yet, and the *yet* is doing important work in the sentence. *Yet* implies a future reveal, in which the thing I have been building privately becomes legible, and the people who had thought I was lost will be shown, gently, that I was not. Beth saying *settled* in the kitchen disrupts that story, because in her version the reveal has already happened, the work is already legible, the verdict is already in and it is favorable. I do not, apparently, want the favorable verdict to arrive ahead of schedule. I want to keep the *yet.*

That is unattractive. I am writing it down anyway. The *yet* is, on inspection, a way of keeping the door open to a bigger eventual story than the one Beth handed me at the counter. If I am *settled* now, in November, in a kitchen, with no noun, then the noun was not the point, and the long imagined arrival — the announcement, the website, the manifesto, the version of the man in the nice shirt that is mine — does not get to be the climax of the year. The climax was, possibly, a Thanksgiving where my sister-in-law thought I seemed okay. That is a smaller climax than I had been holding out for. I am noticing I do not want to trade.

What I would like to do, sitting here, is open the package. Just take Beth's sentence at face value, the way I would want her to take it if I had said it to her about her own life. *You seem really good. Settled. Whatever you're doing, it's working.* Three short clauses from a person with no agenda, on a day she had no reason to flatter me, in a kitchen where I had in fact been doing something — moving between the oven and the stove, talking to Nina about the rolls, intermittently laughing at something Theo said about the parade — that I would, if I saw it from the outside, recognize as the behavior of a person who was, that day, settled. Not every day. That day. The compliment was about that day, and I have been refusing it on behalf of the other days, which is a generosity to my worse moods I cannot justify.

So I am going to take it in, late. *Thanks, Beth.* Three days delayed, in a document she will never read, but on the record, in my own voice, where the voice that issues the verdicts can hear it. I was, on Thursday, in that kitchen, settled. Not solved. Not arrived. Settled, in the sense she meant it, which is the sense of someone who is, for a stretch of an afternoon, in the room they are in. That happened. She saw it. I am allowed to have it.

entries.prompt: When you walked into the house at the end of the day yesterday, what was the first thing you did, and what did the shape of that first ten minutes tell you about what kind of day it had been?
entries.content:
I had not been out of the house long. An hour and ten minutes, the loop with the dog, plus the small detour to drop the book back at Chris's house — he was not home, which I had half-hoped, I left it on the porch with a sticky note that said *finally, thanks,* and the not-home-ness of his house made the whole errand cleaner than it would have been if he had answered the door. So when I say I walked in at the end of the day, I mean I walked in around four-forty, which is what the end of the day is now in late November, the light already mostly gone, the kitchen window doing that thing where it goes from window to mirror over the course of fifteen minutes and you do not notice until you catch yourself in it.

The first thing I did was take my coat off and hang it up. On the hook. Not on the chair, not over the bannister. On the hook. I am writing that down because it is not what I usually do. I usually do the chair, and then later, when I notice the chair has accumulated three coats and a scarf, I do a small remediation pass and put them all on hooks at once, which is its own quiet failure I have been ignoring. Yesterday I put the coat on the hook on the way in, in one motion, without thinking about it, and I noticed I had done it only after I had already walked past it into the kitchen. The body had filed the coat without consulting me.

The second thing I did was unclip the dog and dry her paws with the towel that lives by the back door. She made the small impatient sigh she makes during paw-drying, which is her way of telling me she could be doing something more important, which is a lie because she could not. The third thing was put the kettle on, even though I did not particularly want tea, because the kettle is, I have realized, a thing I do with my hands when I come in. The kettle is the small ceremony I have built in the absence of the airlock. Not as good as the chair upstairs, not as long, not as load-bearing, but a thing. Water in, switch on, the small click and the rising whisper. Four seconds of activity that asks nothing of me and produces a sound I like.

The fourth thing was — and this is where I started to notice the shape — I went to the counter and looked at the notebook. Not to write in it. Just looked. Saw that the top sheet was a list from Saturday, mostly crossed off, with *batteries (AA)* not crossed off because I had forgotten the batteries, again. I tore that sheet off, balled it, threw it out, exposed the next blank page. Did not write anything on the blank page. Walked away from the counter. The dog followed me as far as her water bowl and then stopped, because that is where her interest in my movements ends.

Then I stood at the sink for a minute with the kettle going and looked at the back yard, which was almost completely dark by then, and I thought: this was a fine day. That sentence, in those words. Not *a good day,* which would have been overclaiming. Not *a hard day,* which would have been the older default. *A fine day.* I had taken Nina to school, I had walked, I had read a chapter, I had spent an hour and a half on the document — I will write about that separately, maybe, the document is in a different shape than it was a week ago, I do not want to jinx it by reporting on it too early — and I had taken the dog out and dropped Chris's book and come home. Nothing in the day had distinguished itself. Nothing had broken. Nothing had been performed. And I had walked in and put the coat on the hook without thinking.

The shape of the first ten minutes, I think, was the report. The coat on the hook was the report. The kettle without wanting tea was the report.
