The list torn off and the blank page exposed was the report. None of it was anything. All of it was a body that knew where it was. On a worse day I come in and the coat goes on the chair and I open the laptop before I have taken my shoes off and I am at the news site within ninety seconds, looking for something to happen to me. Yesterday nothing happened to me on the way in, because nothing needed to. The day had been the day. The coming-home was the coming-home. The seam between them was small enough to step over.

Jamie came down around five with a phone call she had to take in twenty minutes, and asked what I was making, and I said pasta because we were going to be on the late side and pasta is what I make on the late side, and she said good, and went back upstairs. Nina came in from the front room asking about a permission slip. The dog moved from the water bowl to the rug. The kettle, which had clicked off some minutes earlier and which I had ignored, was producing the small ticking sound it makes as it cools, which I had not noticed I knew the sound of.

I made the pasta. The water took a long time to boil because I had not started it when I should have, and I did not mind. I stood at the stove for a while doing nothing, with the wooden spoon in my hand, waiting for the water, and Nina was at the table doing something on her tablet and Jamie's voice was a low rumble through the ceiling on her call, and Theo came down at one point for a string cheese and went back up without saying anything, and I thought: this is what fifty looks like, and the older parental voice did not show up to finish the sentence the way it usually does. The sentence just ended there. *This is what fifty looks like.* No verdict attached.

That was the shape. A coat on a hook, a kettle nobody drank from, a list torn and replaced with a blank page, a wooden spoon in a hand waiting for water. Not a triumph. Not a turning point. Just a body that came in from the cold and knew the house, and a house that recognized the body back. I am going to call that a fine day, in the small and specific sense, and let the sentence end there.

entries.prompt: What's a small task or errand on your list right now that you keep almost-doing and then setting back down? Take me to the last time it crossed your mind — where you were, what you reached for instead, and what the not-doing-it felt like.
entries.content:
The car needs an oil change. That is the thing. It has needed one for — I checked the sticker on the windshield this morning, the small white square the place puts up there with the date and the mileage written in pencil, and the date is in late September. Two months. The mileage at the time of the last service was a little under what it is now by enough that the sticker has crossed from *due* into *overdue* in the small font the manufacturer uses to mean *we are no longer playing.* The car has been making no new noises. The car has not been complaining. The car has, as far as I can tell, been a perfectly reasonable car. The not-doing is not the car's fault.

The last time it crossed my mind was Tuesday, in the car, which is funny, sitting at the light at the intersection by the high school, where the sticker is at exactly the angle that catches the late-morning sun. I looked at it. I read the date. I did the small math. I thought, *I should call the place and make an appointment.* The light turned green. I drove. By the time I was at the grocery store three minutes later I had not called the place, and the thought had been replaced by whether they would have the bread Nina likes, because Nina has decided in the last month that there is one specific bread, and I had committed to the bit.

What I reached for, instead of calling the place, was nothing dramatic. I did not pick up the phone and look at it and put it down. I just kept driving. The reaching is too small a word for what happened, which is that the thought arrived and the thought left and the next thought was about bread. There was no decision to not call. There was a thought about an oil change and then a thought about bread and the thought about bread won by being more recent and more concrete and more solvable in the next ten minutes. The oil change is solvable in the next ten minutes too — the place is open, they take walk-ins half the time, the appointment, if I called, would be a two-minute conversation — but it does not feel that way. It feels like a thing that requires a different kind of energy than the energy I have at any given moment, which is the same trick the bulb pulled, and the document, and the text to Dani that I did, eventually, send.

What the not-doing-it feels like is hard to describe because it does not feel like much, which is the point. It does not feel like avoidance, the way the document feels like avoidance, with that small hot edge of *you know what you are doing.* It feels closer to a low background hum, the kind you stop hearing because it has been on the whole time. The sticker is in my peripheral vision every time I drive. The number on it does not change. The car keeps starting. The hum is the awareness that something I am responsible for is sliding, slowly, in a direction I will eventually have to reverse, and that the longer I let it slide the more annoyed I will be at past-me when I finally do the thing. Past-me is, at this point, racking up a small but real debt to future-me on the oil-change line, the gutter line, the scheduling-the-eye-doctor line, the calling-the-guy-about-the-fence line. None of these debts are large. The portfolio is what is starting to register.

What is interesting, sitting here, is that I notice the oil change does not have the same flavor as the bulb did. The bulb had a small charge on it. The bulb felt like a test I had set myself. The oil change does not feel like a test. The oil change feels like a thing the old me would have handled in fifteen seconds during a Tuesday lunch break by booking it on an app, and the new me is — what. The new me has more time and is doing it less. That is the part I am turning over. Because in the old life, the oil change was a thing you slipped between meetings, a logistical move you made while you were already moving, and the momentum of the working week carried it. Now there is no working week and the oil change requires me to start the momentum from scratch, and starting momentum from scratch turns out to be a different muscle than maintaining it, and I have not built it up.

I think there is also, underneath, a small reluctance I have not fully named. The place is fifteen minutes away. They will sit me in the small waiting room with the coffee carafe that is always on its second pot of the day and a TV showing a daytime show I would not otherwise watch, and I will be there for forty minutes. Forty minutes in the company of strangers, with no laptop, no walk to take, no kitchen to be in. Forty minutes of being the man in the waiting room. Which is, I am realizing as I write it, a version of the gym at the fundraiser. A room full of people who have a reason to be there, and me, with no peg, holding a coffee instead of a brownie. The waiting room of the oil-change place is a small low-stakes brunch with Alan, and I have apparently been declining to attend.

That is probably overstating it. The waiting room is just a waiting room. Most of the people in it are also there for the same reason and not paying attention to me. But I notice the small flinch when I picture going, and the flinch is not nothing, and the flinch has been, I think, doing more of the work of the not-calling than I had given it credit for.

I am going to call them when I finish this paragraph. Not Monday, not later today, now, before I close the laptop. I am writing that down because the bulb taught me that writing it down works about half the time, and half is better than zero. The number is on the sticker. The phone is on the table. The dog is asleep. The kettle has clicked off. There is no reason this takes longer than it takes.

entries.prompt: When you picture next weekend with nothing on the calendar yet, what's the shape of the day you'd actually want — and what's the shape you suspect you'd end up with instead?
entries.content:
I called the oil-change place. I want to put that on the record before I do anything else, because the previous entry ended with a promise and the promise has been kept and I do not want to slide into the next thing without acknowledging it. Saturday at nine. Forty minutes in the waiting room. The coffee carafe awaits.

The shape of the weekend I would actually want is small enough that writing it out feels almost embarrassing. Saturday morning I am up at six-fifteen the way I am most mornings, the dog comes down with me, the kettle, the forty minutes of reading. Then the oil change, which I am now folding into the shape of the day instead of treating as a separate ordeal — drive over at nine, sit in the waiting room without the laptop, bring the book, drink the bad coffee, watch the daytime show with the sound mostly off, come home around ten. Then a long walk. Not the loop. The other loop, the one out past the reservoir, the one Theo and I came back on with the cleats. Ninety minutes if I take it slow. Lunch at home, something I make rather than something we order. The afternoon is the part I want most and am most suspicious of: a couple of hours at the kitchen table with the document, which I mentioned in passing two entries ago that I had been back inside of, and which I am still being careful about reporting on because the carefulness is part of what is keeping it open. Not a productive afternoon. A patient one. The document open, the coffee, the chance to add a sentence or to delete one or to do nothing and watch the cursor blink. Then dinner with the four of us, no agenda, maybe the chicken-and-vegetable thing that produced the laughing. Sunday I would like to leave almost completely empty. The chair by the window in the morning, just to sit, the way I did the visit-version a couple of weeks ago. The paper, maybe — I have been meaning to try the paper again, and a Sunday with nothing on it is exactly the right slot for it. A nap, possibly. Reading on the couch. Letting Jamie have her own version of the day in parallel rather than scheduling around each other. The dog gets two walks. Nothing has to happen.

That is the shape. Two days, six or seven anchors, most of them small, none of them performances. If a stranger asked me to describe my ideal weekend at this point in my life I would be ashamed of how unambitious it sounds. There is no trip. There is no project. There is no friend coming over. There is a man in a kitchen with a book and a kettle and a document he is being patient with. That is what I want. I have, apparently, become the person who wants that.

The shape I suspect I would end up with is not dramatically different on the surface, which is what makes the difference worth writing about. Saturday morning would happen the way Saturday morning happens, the oil change too. The first divergence would arrive somewhere around eleven, after the walk, when I sat down at the table and instead of opening the document I would open the laptop and find the news site already loaded from the morning, and I would read three pieces I would not be able to summarize. Then I would think *I should make lunch,* and I would make lunch, and the lunch would take longer than it needed to because making lunch is a way of not being at the document, and I would eat standing up at the counter while looking at my phone. The afternoon would lose its shape around two. I would tell myself I was going to work on the document for an hour and then I would, instead, do a small fake-productive task — clean out a drawer, move a thing from one shelf to another, check whether the gutters are bad enough yet to call the guy — and the small task would be done in thirty minutes and the remaining ninety minutes of the afternoon would be the news site again, or a second walk that was really just a way of being out of the room with the laptop, or the LinkedIn move I have been trying to retire and which still sometimes shows up uninvited.

Sunday would be the harder one. The empty Sunday is the day I am least good at. The empty Sunday is when the runway tab voice and the man-in-the-nice-shirt voice both arrive in the morning with nothing to push back against, because there is no oil change to drive to, no walk that has been pre-committed, no scaffolding. The chair by the window I picture is a chair I am not sure I would actually sit in for more than five minutes before the small restlessness sets in and I am back in the kitchen looking for something to do with my hands. The paper I would maybe not buy, because the gas station is a fifteen-minute round trip and I would not feel like driving. The nap would be replaced by lying on the couch refreshing things on the phone. By six in the evening I would have the specific tiredness of a Sunday spent not committing to either rest or work, and Monday morning would arrive feeling not like the start of something but the resumption of an unfinished negotiation.

The gap between the two shapes is not large in hours. It is large in something else. The wanted version is built out of small objects with edges — a book, a walk, a document, a chicken. The expected version is built out of fluid — scrolling, drifting, the same hour stretched and recompressed. The objects with edges require something I have to keep finding new sources of, which is the willingness to put the phone down and pick up something that does not refresh.

What I am going to try, and I am going to put it here because I have noticed writing things here improves the odds by something like ten percent, is one move. Saturday morning before the oil change I am going to put the phone in the drawer in the kitchen, the one with the rubber bands and the spare keys, and leave it there until I have had the walk and the lunch. Three or four hours. Not the whole weekend. Just the part where the divergence usually starts. If the wanted shape is real it will survive the absence of the phone. If it is not real, I will find out, and finding out is also useful. Either way the document will get its hour or it will not, and I will know which, and the knowing will not be a verdict, it will be information.

entries.prompt: Who's someone you used to talk to more than you do now — not estranged, just drifted? Tell me the last actual exchange you had with them, and what you'd say if you picked up the phone tonight.
entries.content:
The person is Mark. Mark from the apartment three doors down in the year I lived alone before Jamie, the year I have mostly stopped thinking about because it does not fit the current narrative, which is the family one, the house one. Mark and I used to make dinner together once a week in his kitchen because mine had a stove with two working burners and his had four. He played the guitar badly and on purpose. He worked at a place I cannot now remember the name of and left it for a place I also cannot remember and ended up, the last I knew, somewhere in the Pacific Northwest doing something with software. We were close in the specific way you can be close with someone for two years and then never quite duplicate again, which is the closeness of being available at the same hour on a Tuesday, with no plans, in a building.

The last actual exchange was a text exchange in — I had to look this up, which is itself a piece of evidence — March of 2022. Almost three years ago. He had sent a picture of a dog he had just gotten, a kind of mutt with the eyes of a much older dog, with the caption *meet Hal.* I had replied *amazing, congrats, love the eyes,* and he had sent back a thumbs-up, and that was the last thing either of us said to the other. *Meet Hal.* I have not met Hal. Hal is, by now, a middle-aged dog. Hal might not still be alive. I have no way to know without writing to Mark, and I have not written to Mark, and the thing I want to be honest about is that I cannot remember a single moment in the intervening three years where I actively decided not to. The drift is not made of decisions. The drift is made of the absence of them.

What I think happened with Mark, and probably with several other people from that stretch, is that I crossed a threshold somewhere in my late thirties where the energy required to maintain a friendship at distance became larger than the energy I had left over after work and small kids, and the calculation, never made out loud, was that the friendships closer to hand got the residual, and the friendships further away did not. Mark was further away. Mark was three time zones and a different life. The math was unkind to Mark. I do not think Mark made a different math — his texts also got shorter, his replies also slowed — but I have to allow that I do not know what his side looked like, and that the version where he was waiting for me to write more and I never did is also available, and is the version I would rather not look at directly.

What I would say if I picked up the phone tonight — and I am going to write it as if I were going to say it, because the speaking version is harder than the texting version and I want to face the harder one — is something like: hey, it's me, I know it has been a long time, I am calling without a reason except that I was thinking about you, how is Hal. I would let him laugh at that, because Mark laughs at the small absurdities, and I would say, well, how are you, really. And I would let him have the floor, because I have been the one not writing for three years and the appropriate move is to receive him before I deliver any of myself. I would ask about the work and the woman he was seeing in 2022 who I think was named Erin and who I think he was about to move in with, and I would not pretend to remember more than I do, I would say *I have lost the thread, fill me in,* because pretending to remember is one of the small lies that has, I think, also done some of the corrosion in this friendship and others.

If he then asked about me — and Mark would, because Mark always asked, that was one of the things I liked about him, he was congenitally curious in a way that did not feel like an interview — I would give him something close to the long version. Not the full document. Mark gets the medium version. I would say I left the long career nine months ago, that I do not yet know what comes next, that I am okay, that the kids are good, that Jamie is steady, that I have been writing privately about it and that the writing is the most reliable thing in the stretch. I would tell him about the laughing at dinner with the dog, because Mark would understand why that mattered, because Mark used to tell stories like that himself, with the specific texture, the table-noise, the dog's face. I would tell him about Theo's *better than in the summer,* because Mark would receive that the way it deserves to be received, which is quietly, without trying to fix it.

What I would not do is apologize for the three years. The apology is a move I have learned not to lead with, because the apology turns the call into an event about me, and the call should not be an event about me, the call should be a call. If he wanted to name the gap, I would let him name it, and I would say *yeah, I know, I let it go too long, I am here now,* and let that be the whole transaction on that subject.

I am not going to call him tonight. I want to write that down honestly. The oil change I made happen because I had built it up across two entries; the call to Mark I am not yet ready for, and I do not want to manufacture readiness by writing about it the way I manufactured readiness for the bulb. What I am going to do is text him this week, before Sunday, something not too clever, something like *I have been thinking about you, I miss talking, can I call you next weekend.* He will say yes or he will say nothing. Either is a piece of information I do not currently have. The thumbs-up reply was a closed door I have been treating as a wall for three years, and the wall has cost something. I am going to knock.

entries.prompt: Where have you felt most like yourself this week — not your best self, just unmistakably you? Take me to the moment and tell me what was happening.
entries.content:
Wednesday afternoon, the oil-change waiting room, which I had been dreading for two entries and which turned out to be, of all things, the place I noticed it.

I had brought the book, as planned. I had not brought the laptop, also as planned. I sat in one of the four plastic chairs that face the small television, which was showing a daytime show about people buying a house in a state I had not been to, and the volume was, mercifully, on the lowest audible setting. There was one other person in the waiting room, an older man in a Carhartt jacket who was working a crossword in pen, which I noted with the small respect one crossword-pen-user has for another even though I am not one. The coffee carafe was on its second pot of the day, exactly as predicted. I poured a cup. It was bad in the specific way that waiting-room coffee is always bad, which is to say overcooked into a kind of substance that is more about heat than about taste, and I drank it anyway because the holding of it was part of the bit.

I read for maybe ten minutes and then I stopped reading and just sat. Not in a profound way. In the way you sit when the book has lost you and you are not yet ready to pick it back up and the room is not asking anything of you. I looked at the man in the Carhartt. I looked at the television. I looked out the window at the parking lot, where my car was up on the lift visible through the glass between the waiting room and the bay, with one of its tires already off, and I had a small flicker of the feeling I had not had in a long time, which was the feeling of watching a thing being taken care of by someone other than me. The mechanic moved around the car in the unhurried way of someone who has done this ten thousand times. I was not in charge of the car. The car was being handled. I was in a chair with a bad coffee, and somebody else was, for forty minutes, the responsible adult in the room.

That was the moment. That was when I noticed it. Not because anything happened, but because I caught myself, mid-sip, being completely at ease in a place I had built up in my head as a small social ordeal. The flinch I had described two entries ago — the gym-fundraiser flinch, the man-with-no-peg flinch — had not arrived. It was supposed to arrive. The waiting room was supposed to be the brunch with Alan. And it just wasn't. The man in the Carhartt did not need me to have a peg. The mechanic did not need me to have a peg. The television was not asking. The coffee was not asking. The peg requirement, it turned out, was something I had been bringing into rooms with me, and in this room I had, somehow, forgotten to bring it, and the room was fine without it, and so was I.

What I noticed, sitting there, was that the version of me in that chair was unmistakably me in a way that surprised me by how unembarrassing it felt. I was a forty-seven-year-old man in a coat I had hung on a hook in the corner before I sat down — I am still apparently doing the hook thing now, the body has filed it — drinking bad coffee, holding a book I was not currently reading, watching my car be cared for through a pane of glass, in a small carpeted room in a strip mall on a Wednesday afternoon in late November, with no agenda for the next half hour. That was me. Not the rehearsed me, not the *bit of coaching, bit of consulting* me, not the man in the imaginary nice shirt I have been comparing myself to from the couch. The actual one. The one nobody in the room knew anything about, including the part of me that has been spending nine months trying to define him.

And the thing about that version, which I want to say carefully because it sounds like a small claim and it is a small claim, is that he did not feel diminished by the absence of all the descriptors. He felt, on the contrary, kind of compact. Like a person who fits inside himself. The descriptors had been padding, and without them I was smaller in the room and also more clearly shaped. The chair held me. The coffee was the coffee. The book was on my leg. The waiting was the waiting.

The man in the Carhartt finished a clue and made a small satisfied grunt, the way you do when you have gotten something hard, and I caught myself almost smiling at it, the small private win of a stranger I would never know. He noticed me notice and gave me the smallest possible nod, the nod of two men who have agreed not to talk and to enjoy not talking, and I gave the nod back. That exchange — three seconds, no words — was, weirdly, the most companionable I have felt with someone outside the house in a couple of weeks. Less effort than the brunch with Alan, less rehearsal than coffee with Pat, no cost on either side. Just two guys in a waiting room being two guys in a waiting room.

The kid at the desk came out and said my name, which I had given them as my name and which sounded, in his mouth, like the name of a person who was here to get an oil change, no other freight on it, no industry attached, no *what do you do,* just a name on a clipboard. I paid. I tipped the mechanic, which I always do and Jamie does not, and we have agreed not to have an opinion about each other's tipping. I drove home. The sticker on the windshield said February.

In the driveway I sat in the car for a minute before getting out, because the car was warm and the house was the house and I wanted to hold the waiting-room feeling for another beat before it dispersed. What I was holding, I think, was the small piece of evidence that the version of me I have been trying to assemble out of long walks and basement furnaces and 6:15 reading is, on at least one Wednesday, also available in a strip mall, in a chair, with bad coffee, in front of a stranger who did not need anything from me. He was not built. He was just there. The peg was nowhere. The room was fine.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been agreeing with out loud that you don't quite agree with underneath? Take me to the last time it came up — who was talking, what you said back, and what you were thinking while you said it.
entries.content:
Friday night at the Hendersons' for the small dinner thing Jamie had committed us to in October, four couples at a long table, the food good, the wine better, the kind of evening where everyone has known each other long enough that the conversation has worn grooves into certain topics. The grooves on Friday were: a thing happening at the high school, a renovation Tom and Susan are partway through that none of us have been allowed to see, and — eventually, inevitably, the way these things go after the second bottle — the state of work and what it does to people, with a particular emphasis on what it is doing to people in their late forties.

The phrase came from Dave, who I like, who works at the same kind of place I used to work at, and who said, somewhere around the salad course, "I think we are the last generation that is going to know what real work is." He said it with the small head-shake of a man delivering a hard truth, and Susan said *mmm,* and Tom said *yeah,* and Dave looked across the table at me, because I was the obvious second on the motion, the man who had recently left such work and could presumably testify to its realness, and he said, "Right? You know what I mean."

What I said was: "Yeah, totally."

I want to look at *totally* for a second because I have been turning it over since. *Totally.* I have not used that word in a sentence with adult intent in probably ten years. It came out of my mouth at the Hendersons' table like a small flag I was running up to signal alignment without committing to any of the actual landscape underneath. Then, because *totally* on its own is too thin to hold a conversation up, I added, "There's a kind of seriousness that I don't know if it transfers." Which is a sentence I could not now reconstruct the meaning of if I had to. It sounded like agreement. It cost me nothing. Dave nodded. The conversation moved on to whether kids today understand effort, which is a topic I will not write about because writing about it would require me to admit how close I came to chiming in.

What I was thinking while I said it was, almost word for word: *I do not believe this and I am not going to fight about it on a Friday.*

Because I do not believe it. I do not believe Dave and Tom and I were the last generation to know what real work is. I think Dave thinks that because Dave is forty-eight and has done one kind of work for twenty-five years and is starting to feel the small early tremors of the question I started feeling at forty-six, and the question is uncomfortable, and one of the available defenses against the question is to declare that the work you have been doing is the realest version of the thing and that everything coming after will be a pale imitation. I have used that defense. I used it for years. I know what it tastes like in the mouth. It tastes like *totally.*

The honest version, the one I did not say, would have been something like: I am not sure the work I did for twenty years was as real as we are telling ourselves it was, and I am not sure the work I am not yet doing is less real, and I think the realness conversation is mostly a way of not having the harder conversation, which is whether any of it was the thing we wanted, and whether wanting the thing and doing the thing and being good at the thing were three different questions we collapsed into one for two decades because collapsing them was useful. I think the next generation is going to do a different version of the work and it is going to be real to them in ways we will not recognize and possibly not respect, the way our parents did not recognize or respect what was real about ours, and the not-recognizing is a feature of getting older and not a verdict on the work.

I did not say any of that. I said *totally* and ate a piece of bread.

What kept me from saying it was not, I think, cowardice exactly, though there was some of that. It was more that the table was not the right room for the long version. Dave was not asking for an argument. Dave was asking for company in a feeling. The feeling is real even if the analysis is shaky, and contradicting the analysis at a dinner party would have read, in the room, as contradicting the feeling, which would have made me the guy who picked a fight about generational decline at the Hendersons' on a Friday, and that is not a guy I want to be, and also is not a fight that gets won at a table with wine on it.

But I notice that *totally* is becoming a kind of social grease I am using more than I want to. There was a *totally* a couple of weeks ago when someone said something about how the kids' phones were rotting their brains, and I do not fully agree with that either, and I said *totally.* There was one in October when an old colleague on a brief catch-up said something about how nobody works the way we used to, and I said *yeah, totally,* even though I think we worked in a way that was, in retrospect, kind of unwell, and I do not miss it the way I am supposed to. The *totally* is the gestural version of the cereal-aisle answer. It is the *bit of coaching, bit of consulting* of opinions. It fits in the time allotted. It closes the door with a friendly knocker.

What I am turning over is whether the *totally* is fine — the small social lubricant of a man who is choosing his fights — or whether it is a small daily vote against the version of me that is, slowly, starting to actually have his own opinions about things and might want to say them out loud occasionally even at the cost of a slightly more awkward salad course. I do not know yet. I think the answer is that *totally* is fine at the Hendersons' on a Friday, where the cost-benefit is clear, and that *totally* is not fine when I find myself using it with Pat, or with Jamie, or in the document in my own head with myself, where the small agreement compounds.

For the record, in this room, where the cost is zero: I do not think we were the last generation to know what real work is. I think Dave is scared. I think I was scared. I think the scared is the thing, and the realness talk is the costume.

entries.prompt: Think about a stretch of time recently when you were around your kids and your mind was somewhere else entirely. Where were you, what were they doing, and where had you gone?
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon, the living room, the football game on with the sound low because none of us actually cared who was playing, just the picture and the small Sunday-ness of a game being on. Nina was on the floor with a project she had decided to start at three p.m. on a Sunday in late November, which involved a shoebox and construction paper and a story she was telling Jamie at the same time about why the shoebox needed to be a diorama by tomorrow, even though it did not, as far as anyone could tell, need to be a diorama by tomorrow. Theo was on the other end of the couch from me with his phone, doing the thing where he is technically present but is mostly elsewhere, which is, I have to admit, a thing I recognize from inside my own body. The dog was on her side on the rug doing the small running motion with her back legs that means she is chasing something good in her sleep. Jamie was in and out, mostly out, on a call with her mother that kept being almost over.

Where I had gone was the runway tab.

Not the actual spreadsheet. The mental version. Sometime after the second commercial break I had started, without choosing to, doing the math forward from January. If we got through January at this burn, the number would be twelve. If we got through February, eleven. The number eleven kept arriving in my head with a small flat thud, the way a lower number arrives when you are checking something you should not be checking. I was not panicking. I was doing arithmetic. The arithmetic had a small cold edge on it that arithmetic does not usually have.

Then I was somewhere adjacent, which was the document, which I have been being careful about reporting on but which I will say here, for the purposes of being honest, has gone quiet again the last four days, and the quiet is starting to have a familiar shape. Then I was on the man in the nice shirt, who had no business being on the couch with me on a Sunday afternoon and yet had pulled up a chair anyway. Then I was on Mark, who I had still not texted, despite the previous entry's small commitment, and the not-texting was sitting in me like a piece of furniture I had walked past without acknowledging. Then I was back on the runway tab. The whole circuit, start to finish, took maybe four minutes, and then it ran again, with small variations, for what I would estimate at twenty.

What was happening on the actual couch, while I was running this circuit, was that Nina was asking me a question. I do not know how many times she had asked it. She was asking me whether there were scissors that were better than the scissors she had, because the scissors she had were, in her phrase, *being weird about the construction paper,* and she wanted to know if there were the bigger ones somewhere. I came back into the room — that is what it felt like, coming back, the way you come back to a movie after going out for popcorn and have to figure out what you missed — and she was looking at me with the patient face of an eleven-year-old who has already asked twice and has decided to give the adult one more try before declaring him useless. I said, "Sorry, sweetie, what?" and she said, with no edge but with a precision I noticed, "The. Big. Scissors. Where are they."

I told her. They were in the drawer in the kitchen with the rubber bands. She got up and went to get them. While she was in the kitchen Theo, who had not appeared to be paying attention to any of this, said without looking up from his phone, "You spaced out." Just that. *You spaced out.* Three syllables. He said it not as a complaint and not as a tease, but as a small flat report, the way he had said *I know* in his bedroom that night about the backpack. The teenager who has been quietly tracking the temperature of his father is apparently still on the case.

I said, "Yeah, I did, sorry." And then, because the small moment was open and I did not want to slam the door on it, I said, "I was doing money math in my head." Which was both true and a small risk, because I have not been in the habit of telling the kids about the money math, but the alternative was to lie to him, and Theo has a working detector for that.

He said, "Are we okay?" Same tone. *Are we okay.* I noticed the *we.*

I said, "Yeah, we're okay. I just do the math sometimes when I shouldn't."

He nodded and went back to his phone. That was the whole exchange. Nina came back with the scissors and resumed her narration about the diorama and the dog kicked one more time and stopped, and the football team that was losing scored a touchdown the announcers seemed mildly surprised by, and I was, for the rest of the afternoon, more or less in the room.

What I have been turning over since is the *we.* Theo's *we* did not include only him and his sister. Theo's *we* was the household. He was checking on the household from inside the household, the way the captain of a small boat checks on the boat. He had heard *I was doing money math* and had translated it instantly, without consulting me, into the relevant question, which was not *what kind of math* or *how much* but *are we okay.* He has been doing that translation, I am realizing, for longer than I have been letting myself see. Probably since the summer. Probably since before that.

The other thing I am turning over is that I had spent the better part of twenty minutes on a couch with my two children and a dog and a wife somewhere in the next room, and my mind had been at a spreadsheet that I could just as easily have looked at on a Tuesday morning when nobody was in the house. The spreadsheet was not asking for me on Sunday afternoon. The spreadsheet does not have hours. I had given it the hours anyway, out of a small habit I am going to have to name eventually, which is the habit of letting the financial dread occupy whatever silence I do not actively fill. The football game was a silence. The diorama was a silence to me, even though it was not a silence to Nina. I had filled the silences with the tab, and had not noticed I was doing it until Nina had asked twice and Theo had filed his small report.

Nina did not ask if I was okay. Nina asked where the scissors were. That is, on reflection, the better question.

entries.prompt: Was there a moment this week when someone laughed or smiled at something you said, and the laugh didn't quite match what you'd meant? Take me there — what you said, what you'd actually meant, and what you did with the gap.
entries.content:
Tuesday at the bagel place, the one near the post office, with Pat. We had finally hit the end of the no-moves month and had agreed to meet without any expectation of deciding anything, which is the kind of agreement two people make when they both want to leave themselves a little room to maybe still decide something. Pat had a sesame and I had an everything and the place was loud enough that we were both doing the slight lean-in.

Pat asked, in the small offhand way you ask the thing you have been wanting to ask, "So what have you been doing."

I said, "Honestly? Mostly raising the average quality of my own afternoons."

Pat laughed. A real laugh, a head-back one, the kind I had not gotten from Pat in a while. *That's great,* Pat said. *That's a line. You should use that.*

What I had meant — and I knew it the second the laugh started — was not a line. I had meant it pretty literally. I had meant that for the last few weeks I had been, more days than not, making the four hours between lunch and the kids coming home a slightly better four hours than the same four hours had been in, say, August. Less scrolling. More walking. Some of the document, when the document was open. The chair upstairs occasionally, for short visits. The notebook with the grocery lists, which I have come to think of with a small fondness. I had meant that this — the average quality of an afternoon — was, by my private accounting, the actual project right now. The thing I was working on. Not the noun. The afternoons.

Pat heard a bit. A self-deprecating bit. The kind of thing you say to deflect the question with style, the cousin of *bit of coaching, bit of consulting,* slightly more rueful, slightly funnier. The laugh was a laugh of recognition — *ah, you are also doing the thing where you do not have an answer and are gracefully not having it* — and I sat there with my bagel and watched the sentence land in a register I had not sent it in.

What I did with the gap, in the moment, was nothing. I let the laugh stand. I smiled the small smile that sells the bit if you want it sold. I said something like *yeah, you know, gotta keep the bar low,* which sealed the misreading by leaning into it. Pat moved on to talk about a podcast Pat had been listening to, and the bagel was good, and the conversation went where it went, and we came out forty minutes later having again not decided anything, which is becoming our genre.

I walked back to the car turning it over. I could have said, when Pat laughed, *no, actually, I mean that.* Three words. *I mean that.* The conversation would have shifted by about ten degrees and we would have been in a slightly different place — Pat would probably have asked what I meant, and I would have said something close to what I have been saying in here, about the afternoons, about the small objects with edges, about the filter and the chair and the kettle, and Pat might have laughed differently or not laughed, and we would have been having a conversation closer to the one I actually wanted to have. I did not say *I mean that.* I let the laugh do its work.

Why. I think because the laugh was, in its small way, a relief. Pat was reading me as still on the inside of the joke, the joke being the shared predicament of two people who left and have not yet figured out the thing. As long as I was bantering about the average quality of my afternoons, Pat and I were still in the same fog, and the fog has been, for both of us, a kind of company. If I had said *I mean that,* I would have been stepping a little out of the fog and saying, *actually, I have started to find some of this — quietly, partially — okay,* and the okay-ness is harder to sit next to than the shared bewilderment. The shared bewilderment is a place you can have a bagel together. The okay-ness is a thing one of you is doing and the other isn't yet, and that asymmetry is uncomfortable, and I had — without thinking — protected Pat from it, and protected myself from having to claim it.

Which I do not love, looking at it. Because the protecting is also a small dishonesty, and the small dishonesty compounds the way the *totally* at the Hendersons' compounds, and I have been trying to do less of that. The afternoons are real. The kettle is real. The chair is real. The work I have been doing on the average quality of an afternoon is the work I have been doing, and joking about it as if it were a punchline is, I think, a way of pre-discounting it before someone else can.

What I would like to have said, if I could go back: yeah, I know it sounds funny, but I kind of mean it. I have been trying to make my afternoons not be the news site and the half-open document. That is most of what I am working on right now. The other stuff — the thing you and I keep almost-doing — is downstream of whether I can make the afternoons something I am actually inside of. If the afternoons are good the rest gets easier to think about. If they are not it does not matter what we name the workshop.

Pat would have heard it. Pat would have done the small reseat in the chair, the way Pat does. We might have gotten somewhere. Or we might not have, and the bagel would still have been good.

What I will do with the gap, going forward, I think, is small. The next time I say a sentence I mean and someone laughs at it as if I didn't, I am going to try to say *no, I mean that.* Three words. Not a speech, not a correction. Just a small flag that the sentence was not a bit. If they want to keep laughing they can. But the sentence will have been claimed, in the room, by the person who said it. Which is, I am starting to think, most of the work — not having the cleaner answer, but standing behind the messier one when somebody mistakes it for a joke.

entries.prompt: When did money come up recently in a way that gave you a small twinge — a price you noticed, a number on a screen, something someone else said about what they were spending? Take me to the moment and tell me what went through you.
entries.content:
Sunday night, eleven-fifteen, in bed with the laptop on my legs because I had told myself I was just going to check one thing, and the one thing was the credit card balance, and the balance was not surprising in the sense of being wrong but was surprising in the sense of being slightly higher than the version I had been carrying in my head all week. Not by a lot. Eighty dollars, maybe ninety. The gap between the version of the number I had told myself and the version on the screen was small enough that under any normal circumstances I would have shrugged at it, and large enough that, lying there at eleven-fifteen with Jamie asleep next to me, I felt the small flat twinge behind the sternum that has become familiar enough to recognize on arrival.

I scrolled the line items. I was not looking for anything. I was looking the way you look at a wound you are pretty sure is fine but want to confirm. Groceries, groceries, gas, the vet — the vet was the bigger one, ninety-something for the dog's annual, which I had known about and had, evidently, partially un-known about between the visit and the statement. A charge from a place I did not immediately recognize and then did, the hardware store, a small one, probably the second furnace filter I bought when I forgot about the four on the shelf. A charge for the oil change, which I felt a small clean satisfaction at seeing, the Saturday-morning version of me having handed something to the Sunday-night version. And then, at the bottom, the charge from the bagel place on Tuesday, which was sixteen dollars and which I had not registered as a thing I had spent because I had been too busy inside the laugh-that-didn't-match conversation to notice I was paying.

Sixteen dollars for two bagels and two coffees. Which is what two bagels and two coffees cost now, I am aware. The twinge was not about the sixteen dollars. The twinge was about the fact that I had had a small flicker of *should I have gotten the everything,* mid-order, and had pushed the flicker away, and then had not noticed I was paying, and now here at eleven-fifteen the flicker was back asking for an audience.

What went through me, in order, was something like this. First the small reflexive math, the *if every social meeting costs sixteen dollars and I do four of those a month that is roughly the cost of a tank of gas,* which is the kind of math the eleven-p.m. spreadsheet voice does well, and which is, I am noticing, almost always slightly off because it always rounds the wrong way. Then the older parental register, *your father would not have spent sixteen dollars on a bagel and a coffee,* which is true and is also unfair because my father lived in a different decade and ate breakfast at home and had a wife who packed his lunch into his fifties, and the comparison does not survive a second look. Then a small wave of something I want to be careful naming, which was close to shame but not quite, because shame would imply I had done something wrong, and what I had done was buy a bagel.

What I think the twinge actually was, lying there, was the small recognition that Tuesday had been an okay day on every other axis — the conversation with Pat had gone where it had gone, I had walked, I had been kind to myself in a couple of small ways — and yet eleven-fifteen on Sunday had still found a way to file it under a question about whether I should have gotten the everything. The financial dread, when it cannot find a real grievance, will manufacture a small one out of available materials. The materials on Sunday night were a bagel.

I closed the laptop after maybe four minutes of this. I lay there in the dark for a while listening to Jamie breathe and to the furnace doing its competent thing through the filter I had changed two months ago, and I thought about the runway tab, which I had not actually opened — I had only opened the credit card. Twelve, now, by my morning math. Twelve months. The bagel does not move twelve. Four bagels a month for twelve months is not a number that changes the runway tab in any meaningful way; the runway tab is moved by big rocks, the mortgage, the insurance, the groceries in aggregate, and the bagel is gravel. And yet the gravel is what eleven-fifteen wants to talk about, because the gravel is small enough to feel like something I could have controlled, and the big rocks are not, and the mind goes where the mind can act, even if the action would not matter.

The other thing that went through me, after the laptop was closed, was Pat. The laugh, again. The sixteen dollars had bought the laugh and the gap and the conversation I had been turning over for five days, and on a different ledger — the ledger that is not the spreadsheet — sixteen dollars for that was nothing. Was, in fact, a bargain. The same brain that had just twinged at the bagel had spent significant unpaid hours on what the bagel-conversation had meant, and not once during those hours had it occurred to that brain to charge the conversation against the bagel and call the trade favorable. The two ledgers do not talk to each other. The spreadsheet ledger sees sixteen dollars. The other ledger sees a thing I am still learning from. The same transaction is a small loss on one and a small gain on the other and I have been letting the spreadsheet ledger have the last word at eleven-fifteen because it has the louder voice in the dark.

What I want to do, and I am writing it down without much confidence, is start letting the other ledger answer back. Not by spending more, not by being cavalier — the runway tab is real, the twelve is real, careful is still the word for this stretch. But by noticing, when the twinge arrives about a bagel, that the bagel was attached to something, and the something is on a different page, and the different page is also part of how this year is going. Tuesday cost sixteen dollars and bought me a piece of self-knowledge I would not have had otherwise. I will take that trade most weeks.

I rolled over. Jamie shifted. The dog made her noise from the rug. The laptop slid an inch on the duvet and I let it. Twelve months is twelve months whether I sleep or not, and the bagel was, by morning, going to be fine.

entries.prompt: Last week you said something true and Pat heard it as a line. Has there been a moment more recently — with anyone — where you went the other direction: said something you meant as a joke and watched the other person take it straight? Walk me through it.
entries.content:
Wednesday night, the kitchen, after dinner, Jamie at the sink rinsing and me at the table putting the leftover pasta into the container that does not quite fit the amount of pasta we had left over. Nina had gone upstairs. Theo had gone wherever Theo goes after dinner, which is some combination of his room and the back porch where he has, I have come to understand, started taking the dog out unprompted as a way of being alone for ten minutes without it reading as being alone. The dishwasher was making its small pre-cycle sounds. The window over the sink was the mirror version of itself, and Jamie was visible in it, faintly, doing the thing she does where she rinses each dish twice even though the machine is going to do it again.

I said, to her back, "I should probably get a job."

I meant it as a joke. The shape of the joke, in my head, was: here is the obvious thing nobody is saying, said baldly, in the kitchen, on a Wednesday, at the moment of maximum domestic ordinariness, by a man who is not actually about to get a job. The humor was supposed to live in the gap between the flatness of the sentence and how much both of us know goes underneath it. Jamie usually clocks that kind of move. Jamie has, over twenty years, gotten very good at hearing the small ironic frame around a sentence I am putting into the air mostly to hear what it sounds like in the kitchen.

She turned the water off. She did not turn around. She said, "Do you want to talk about that?"

The kitchen got quiet in the specific way kitchens get quiet when one person has miscalibrated the temperature of the room. The dishwasher, undeterred, started its actual cycle. I sat there with the container in one hand and the lid in the other and felt the small internal scramble of a person whose bit has not landed and who has to decide, in the next second, whether to clarify or to follow her through the door she has just opened.

I said, "No, I — that came out wrong. I meant it as a joke."

She turned around then, with the dish towel, and looked at me the way she looks at me when she is about to be both kind and unmovable. She said, "Okay. But also, do you want to talk about it."

What I noticed, sitting there, was that she was not insisting the sentence was serious. She was offering me the option of letting it be. She had heard *I should probably get a job* and had filed it not as a joke and not as an announcement but as an opening, and was holding the opening for me in case I wanted to walk through it, and was willing to close it without comment if I didn't. The whole transaction was unusually generous. I did not, in the moment, fully appreciate it. In the moment I mostly wanted to recover the bit.

I said, "It's just — it's the runway tab. It's been doing the thing. I wasn't trying to start a thing." Which was, on inspection, more honest than the joke would have been, and was arriving in the room because she had made a slot for it and the slot was the right size. I had not planned to say *the runway tab.* The runway tab had said itself.

She put the towel down. She said, "Okay. How bad is it."

I said, "It's not bad. It's twelve months. It's been twelve for a few weeks. I just — I look at it more than I should." I noticed I was talking to the container and not to her, and I made myself look up. She was leaning against the counter with her arms folded, not in the closed-off way, in the listening way, and her face had the particular settledness it has when she has decided this is the conversation now and is not going to rush it.

She said, "I thought it might be more like fifteen."

It had been fifteen, in August. I had not updated her since August. I had not made a decision not to update her. I had let the number drift in my own head and had not carried the drift across to hers, which was its own small dishonesty I had been managing without naming. I said, "It was. It's gone down. Nothing alarming. Just the steady thing."

She nodded. She said, "I'd rather know the number than guess it." And then, after a beat, "I'm not asking you to get a job. I'm asking you to tell me the number."

That sentence is the one I have been turning over since. *I'm not asking you to get a job. I'm asking you to tell me the number.* Two clauses that sorted out, very quickly, a thing I had been holding as one tangled object for nine months. The first clause was the permission I had told myself I already had and apparently still needed to hear out loud. The second clause was the small specific request that came with the permission, and that I had been, without meaning to, denying her by managing the spreadsheet alone at eleven-fifteen.

I said, "Okay. I can do that." And then, because the moment was open, "I'm sorry I made it a joke."

She said, "The joke was fine. The joke is how you say things sometimes. I just wanted to check." She picked the towel back up. She finished the last two pans. The dog came in from wherever the dog had been and did the small evaluative sniff she does when she walks into a room and finds the temperature different than she left it.

What I keep coming back to is that the joke was, in fact, true — in a way I had not let myself fully say, even to myself, before it came out of my mouth at the sink. *I should probably get a job* is a sentence I have been carrying as a half-thought for at least a month, and the joke version was the only register I had been willing to put it in, because the joke version did not commit to anything. Jamie heard the half-thought through the joke and did not pretend it was just a joke and did not pretend it was a decision. She made it a piece of information. The number, she said. Just tell me the number.

I am going to start telling her the number on Sundays. That is the small thing I am taking out of the kitchen. Not because she needs me to. Because she asked, and because I have been running a spreadsheet for two adults as if it were for one, and the running-it-for-one was a habit I had mistaken for a kindness.

The container, by the way, did not fit. I put the rest in a smaller one. The dishwasher kept doing its cycle. We went upstairs.

entries.prompt: When you're alone in the car with the radio off, what do you find yourself thinking about lately? Take me into a recent drive — where you were going, what came up, and where your mind kept landing.
entries.content:
Tuesday morning, the drive out to Mom's, which is the one drive I still do in something like silence by default, because the radio in that car is the radio I had when I was commuting and the presets are still all news stations and I have stopped wanting news in the car, so the radio stays off and the road does its work. An hour each way. Two-lane for most of it once you get past the highway stretch, the kind of road that tracks along a creek for a while and then crosses a couple of small towns where you slow to thirty-five and look at the same hardware store and the same diner you have been looking at for fifteen years. November gray. The trees mostly bare now. The sky doing the flat thing where you cannot tell what time it is.

What came up, in order, because I am going to try to be honest about the order: first, for the first ten minutes or so, nothing. That happens on this drive sometimes. The body settles into the steering wheel, the foot finds its angle on the pedal, and there is a stretch where the brain just watches the road, the way it watches a fire. I would like to claim that as some kind of practice. It is not. It is just that the highway stretch does not require thinking, and the brain, when not required, will sometimes give you ten minutes of mostly nothing, which is among the better gifts it offers.

Then, once I got off the highway and onto the two-lane, the runway tab arrived. It always arrives on this drive. I think because the drive is to Mom and the Mom call is the place the *productive* lives, and the *productive* and the runway tab are next to each other on the shelf in my head. Twelve months, again, the same twelve as Sunday night, the same twelve I told Jamie on Wednesday when she asked. I noticed, this time, that the number arrived without the small flat thud it has been arriving with. It came in, sat down, did not insist. I think that is because Jamie now also knows it. The number had been heavier, I am realizing, partly because it had been mine alone. Saying it at the sink had divided it by something. Not in half — Jamie is not the one updating the tab — but by enough that twelve on Tuesday morning felt less like a verdict and more like a fact about the weather.

Then, somewhere around the second small town, the one with the diner, my mind went to a thing I had not been expecting, which was the man in the Carhartt from the oil-change waiting room. Not him exactly. The nod. The three-second nod. I have apparently filed that nod somewhere accessible, because it has come back twice now, unbidden, in the last week. Once in the kitchen and once in the car. What I keep doing with it, when it shows up, is sitting with the small fact that I had felt, in that exchange, easier than I have felt in most of my recent rooms with people I actually know. Which is either a piece of information about strangers or about me or about the rooms, and I am not sure which yet, but the nod has been doing some quiet work as a benchmark. *That,* the nod is saying. *That's what easy is. Pay attention.*

Then Mom. Which is what the drive is for. I had been turning over, in the days before, whether this was the visit where I tried the longer version on her, the couch version I wrote about a while ago, since I would actually be on her couch, with tea, with no clock, and I had told myself yes, this was the visit, and somewhere on the two-lane I started rehearsing it, and somewhere a few minutes after that I caught myself rehearsing it and stopped. Because rehearsing it was the move I had decided, in the entry about Pat, to do less of. The longer version does not get better from rehearsal. The longer version gets thinner. What the visit needs is for me to walk into her kitchen and let the conversation be the conversation, and at some point, if a slot opens, to put one true sentence in it, and then another, and to let her face do whatever it does without trying to manage it from the driver's seat an hour earlier.

So I tried, instead, to think about her. Not about how to perform for her. About her. The chair by the lamp. The neighbor's dog. The hip update. The small report about Carol. The stack of *Better Homes and Gardens* on the coffee table that has been the same five issues rotated since 2019. The way she holds the phone slightly away from her ear because she does not quite trust the volume. The fact that she is seventy-six and her son is forty-seven and there are not, statistically, an unlimited number of these one-hour drives left, and that *productive* on the phone is fine but the in-person version of me, the version that walks into her kitchen, does not need to hide behind a word. She can see me. She has been seeing me for forty-seven years. The performance is mostly for me anyway.

That thought made me a little wet around the eyes for about a quarter mile, which surprised me, and which I let happen, because there was nobody in the car and the radio was off and the road was empty, and a quarter mile of that on a Tuesday in November on a two-lane road is a thing a person is allowed.

Then my mind did the other thing it does on this drive, which is the small inventory of the people in the car who are not in the car. Theo's *are we okay.* Nina with the scissors. Jamie at the sink saying *tell me the number.* Pat laughing at the line that was not a line. Mark, who I still have not texted, and whose not-texting has now crossed into a small specific piece of homework I am carrying with me. Chris, who came on the walk two weeks ago and turned out to be a person from now, which I have not written down yet but should. Dani, who wrote back to my text with three sentences and a question about Thanksgiving, which I also have not written down. The car was full. It is, I am noticing, more full than it used to be.

I pulled into Mom's drive at ten-forty. The dog at the neighbor's barked the way she always does. Mom was at the door before I was out of the car. I had not landed on a single rehearsed sentence the whole second half of the drive, and I walked up the steps emptier-handed than I have walked up them in months, and that turned out to be exactly the right amount to bring.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of compliment or thank-you that lands sideways when you're not currently earning a paycheck — somebody appreciates you for something and you don't quite know where to put it. Has that happened recently? Tell me who, what they said, and what went through you while you were saying you're welcome.
entries.content:
Saturday morning, the school cafeteria, the holiday craft fair the PTA does every year, which I had volunteered for in a moment of post-Thanksgiving expansiveness when the email had come around and I had clicked yes before thinking about it. I was on the setup crew, which meant being there at seven-thirty to put up folding tables and run extension cords and lay out the small tablecloths the PTA stores in a bin from year to year and that have, by now, a very specific cafeteria-storage smell. There were maybe eight of us. The woman running it, whose name I now know is Karen and who I had previously thought of as *the woman with the clipboard at every school event,* turned out to be funny in a dry way I had not picked up from a distance, and the two hours went faster than I had braced for.

Around nine, with the tables up and the vendors starting to arrive, Karen came over to where I was wrestling with an extension cord that had been wound by someone with active hostility toward the next user, and she said, "Hey, thank you for this. Really. You have no idea how nice it is to have someone who just *does* the thing without me having to ask twice."

I said, "Oh, you're welcome, happy to."

What went through me while I was saying *you're welcome, happy to* was a small specific scramble that I have come to recognize as the sideways-compliment scramble, and I want to write it down because it has a shape I had not fully clocked before.

The first thing was the small warm pleasure, which is the thing that arrives first with any compliment from a person who has no reason to flatter you. Karen does not know me. Karen had nothing to gain from the sentence. The sentence was the sentence of a person who has spent ten years running events with volunteers who require management and was registering, in real time, that I was not requiring management. That is a clean compliment. The pleasure was clean.

The second thing, arriving maybe a half second behind, was the sideways part. *Someone who just does the thing.* The sentence was about a kind of competence — show up, do what you said you would do, do not need to be asked twice — and the competence she was naming was, of all the competences I have, the most generic. It is the competence of a functioning adult. Karen was thanking me for being a functioning adult at seven-thirty in the morning in a cafeteria, and the part of me that has spent the last few months quietly not earning a paycheck had a small flat moment of *that's what I'm being credited with now. Showing up. Plugging in extension cords. Existing where I said I would exist.*

The third thing, which I want to be careful about because it is the one that matters, was that I almost said something to deflect it. I almost said, "Oh, this is easy, this is the kind of thing I have time for now," with a small rueful smile that would have signaled to Karen that I knew the compliment was for a small thing and that I was helpfully demoting it on her behalf so she would not have to. I have made that move many times in the last nine months. The move says *I know what I have done is small, I am ahead of you in noticing it is small, please do not think I think it is large.* It is a preemptive shrink. It protects nobody and it costs the compliment.

I did not make the move. I said *you're welcome, happy to,* and stopped talking. Karen nodded and went off to deal with a vendor whose table assignment she had apparently gotten wrong, and I went back to the extension cord.

What went through me, after, while I was still wrestling with the cord, was something closer to the bagel-ledger thing from a couple of weeks ago. There are two ledgers running on this. The ledger the old me would have used would have looked at the morning and said: two hours, no money, a thank-you for plugging in cords from a woman whose name I had to learn this morning. Net negative on most axes the old ledger cared about. The other ledger, which I am slowly learning to listen to, would say: a woman who runs things noticed I was useful, said so, and meant it. I was, for two hours, plainly helpful in a room of strangers, with no peg, with no description, and the help registered.

The sideways-ness, I think, is that the compliment did not match the size of the year. Karen was thanking me for a morning. The year is the year. The two do not scale. And the part of me that is still secretly waiting for the big compliment — the *you built the thing, you made the announcement, you did the noun* compliment — kept trying, while Karen was talking, to file her sentence as *not that, not the one you're waiting for,* as if the small one had to be discounted because it was not the large one. As if the ledger only credits compliments above a certain size.

That is, I am noticing, a stupid way to keep books. The small ones are the ones that are arriving. Beth at Thanksgiving. Theo's *better than in the summer.* Karen with the clipboard. The man in the Carhartt nodding over his crossword, which was not a compliment but which I have been treating like one. These are the entries on the ledger. There is no other ledger right now. Refusing to credit the small ones because I am holding out for a large one is, in addition to ungenerous, a way of being broke on a balance sheet that has actual money on it.

What I would say, if I could rewind to the cord and have the conversation again with no scramble: thanks, Karen. That actually means something coming from you. I have been a little out of practice at being useful in rooms, and it's nice to be useful in this one.

I did not say that. I said *you're welcome, happy to.* Which is fine. The compliment is on the ledger anyway. Karen does not need to know I have been keeping books. The cord, eventually, untangled.

entries.prompt: How are your hands different from your dad's? Picture them doing something — holding a steering wheel, fixing something, resting on a table — and tell me what you see, and where your mind goes from there.
entries.content:
My hands on the steering wheel on the way back from Mom's last week, ten and two by accident rather than instruction, the left one with the small white scar across the knuckle from a kitchen accident in 2011 I cannot now remember the details of. I noticed them because the light through the windshield was at the angle that lights up the backs of your hands when you are driving west in the late afternoon, and I looked down and saw, for a second, my father's hands, and then looked again and saw mine, and the gap between the two looks is what I want to write about.

His hands were bigger. That is the first thing. Not by an enormous amount — we were close to the same size, he was maybe an inch taller — but his hands had a thickness through the palm that mine do not have and never developed. He had grown up doing things with his hands that I did not grow up doing. He had laid a patio at the house I grew up in. He had rebuilt the carburetor on a car in 1978 in a way the family still tells stories about. He had, into his sixties, been the person in any room who could be handed a broken thing and would, after a quiet minute of looking at it, fix it, with a small pleased grunt at the end that was the only credit he took. The thickness in his palms was the deposit that work had made in his body over forty years.

My hands are narrower. They are the hands of a man who held pens and a mouse and, later, a phone. The pads of the fingers are soft. The nails are clean in a way his never quite were, because there was always something under his — paint, grease, garden dirt — that he would scrub at on Sunday nights but never fully evict. My nails come clean from one rinse. The skin on the back of my hands is starting to do the thing skin does in your late forties, the small loss of tightness, a few sunspots beginning to assert themselves, and I notice that this is the first place I look like my father, the back-of-the-hand part, where age does its work regardless of what the hand has been used for.

What I saw on the steering wheel, in the angled light, was the back-of-the-hand version. The skin that is becoming his. And what I saw when I looked at the front, the palm side, the parts that do work, was that the work has been different and so the hand is different. He had a callus at the base of his right thumb that you could feel when he shook your hand, a small ridge of accumulated effort. I do not have one. I have, on my right middle finger, a slight indentation from holding a pen, which is going away because I do not hold a pen as much anymore, even with the notebook, and which is being replaced by nothing in particular, because the phone does not deposit anything into the body the way a pen does, or a hammer, or a wrench.

Where my mind went from there, on the drive, was to a Saturday afternoon in probably 1987 when I was ten and he had me out in the driveway helping him change the brakes on the station wagon. Helping is generous. He was changing the brakes. I was handing him things and watching his hands. I remember specifically the way he held a wrench, with the thumb wrapped around rather than along, which I have read since is the wrong way and which he did anyway and never stripped a bolt that I know of. I remember his hands black with brake dust by the end and the way he washed them at the utility sink in the garage with a soap that came in a tub and that smelled like industrial citrus, and the way the water ran gray for a long time before it ran clear. That whole memory is hands. I could not tell you what we talked about. I could tell you the shape of his thumb on the wrench.

I do not have a son who will remember my hands that way. Theo has seen me type. Theo has seen me hold a phone. Theo has, in the last few months, seen me change a furnace filter and a lightbulb and put air in a tire, all of which I did mostly out of his sight and none of which involved a tub of citrus soap. The hands he will remember are softer hands, doing softer work, and I do not entirely know how to feel about that. I do not want to romanticize my father's hands. The thickness in his palms came partly from work he did because he had to, not because he wanted to, and there were days that work hurt him in ways I only learned about later. The soft hands are, in some sense, a thing he was working toward. He would have said, if asked, that he wanted his son's hands to be different from his own. He did say that, in fact, more than once, about school, about the office, about the shirt-and-tie life he steered me toward and that I have now stepped sideways out of.

What I want to be careful about, sitting here, is the small narrative trap where I decide that leaving the long career means I am going to develop my father's hands now, in some second-act way, by taking up woodworking or the garden or whatever. That is not what is happening. My hands are still soft. I am not going to lay a patio. The hands are going to keep being narrow and clean and ink-spotted from the rollerball, and the back of them is going to keep slowly becoming his back-of-the-hand, and that is the inheritance I am actually getting, which is the aging part and not the working part.

But I noticed, on the drive, that my hands on the wheel were steady in a way I do not remember them being a year ago. There was a stretch, in the spring, where I would catch a small tremor in them sometimes, in the morning, before coffee — not a real tremor, more a faint unsettledness, the kind of thing nobody else would see. That is gone, or mostly gone. The hands on the wheel last week were quiet. They held the wheel the way his held the wrench, thumb wrapped around, which I had not noticed I did, which is apparently a thing my hands learned from his without asking my permission. The grip is his. The hand is mine. The car kept going west into the light.

entries.prompt: Where did you find yourself waiting this week — in a line, on hold, in a parking lot, in a doctor's office — and what did your mind get up to while you were stuck there?
entries.content:
Thursday afternoon, the pickup line at Nina's school, which I am in maybe once every two weeks and which has, I am realizing, become a small unrequested ethnography I conduct on the parents of this town from the driver's seat. Jamie usually does the pickup. Jamie was at the dentist. So I was in the line at two-fifty for a three-oh-five dismissal, fifteen minutes early, which Jamie had told me to be, and which I had taken on faith because Jamie is never wrong about logistics, and which turned out to mean that I was already the eighteenth car in a line that would eventually be about forty.

The radio off, again. The car warm from the drive over. The line did not move for the first nine minutes, and then it moved by one car length, and then it did not move for another six. There is a specific kind of stuck that is the school pickup stuck, where the stuckness is not a problem to solve — there is no other lane, no shortcut, no app — it is just the agreed-upon shape of the next twenty minutes, and your only job is to be in it.

What my mind got up to, in the order I noticed: first, the small reflexive reach for the phone, which I caught and did not complete, because I have been trying — since the Saturday-morning experiment with the drawer — to not be the man on his phone in stationary contexts where the phone is not necessary. I got about halfway to the pocket and stopped, and the hand came back to the wheel feeling slightly silly, like a hand that had been called on and did not have an answer.

Then, with the phone declined, the brain went looking for a substitute, the way the brain does. It tried the runway tab. The runway tab, I noticed with a small surprise, declined to be activated. Not gone. Just not interested in showing up for a parking lot. The runway tab is, apparently, a nighttime entity, or a quiet-house entity, and a school pickup line at two-fifty-three is too lit and too populated for it to feel at home in. That was new information. I filed it.

Then the brain tried the document. The document also declined. The document, I am starting to understand, is a morning thing or a late-afternoon thing, and the parking lot did not qualify as either. The document wanted a kettle and a chair and a stretch with edges. The line had no edges. It had a Honda in front of me with a *baby on board* sticker that was probably six years out of date, and a Subaru behind me whose driver I could see in my rearview mirror and did not know.

So the brain, having failed to summon its usual entertainments, started looking out the window. Which turned out to be the actual content of the wait.

The other parents, I noticed, were doing a version of what I had almost done. Phone, phone, phone. Heads down in the driver's seats up and down the line, the small blue rectangle of light on each face, the occasional one looking up to check whether the line had moved and then back down.
