I am not going to be sanctimonious about this — I have been that head-down face many times, including in this same line — but I noticed it the way you notice a thing you have just declined to do. There were maybe three of us not on phones. The woman in the minivan two cars up was reading what looked like a paperback. A man in a pickup four cars back had the window cracked and was just looking at the school, with the patient face of someone who had decided this was his break, not his interruption. The woman and the man did not know each other and were not signaling to each other, but they were, in some quiet way, in the same room. The phone people were each in their own private room. Same parking lot, two different occupancies.

I thought about the man in the Carhartt. The nod. I thought maybe I would catch the eye of the pickup-truck man if the line moved enough to put us briefly side by side, and I would do the small nod and see if he did it back. I did not, in the end, get aligned with him before the line lurched again, but I held the small intention to, which felt friendly even unfulfilled.

Then my mind did the thing it has been doing on drives, the small inventory. Not a worry inventory. A check-on. Mom is fine, the visit went well, the couch version mostly happened, she had made the tea. Theo is — Theo is fourteen, I do not know, but the cleats are by the door and he is wearing them, which is a kind of report. Nina is about to come out of the building in the next twelve minutes with whatever the day put on her. Jamie at the dentist, presumably mid-cleaning, head tipped back, in a chair I cannot picture clearly because the only dentist's chair I can picture clearly is mine. Pat I owe a follow-up to. Mark I owe a text to, still, embarrassingly. Dani I am, surprisingly, in the middle of a thread with that has gone four messages back and forth and is, by the modest standards of our last few years, an active correspondence. Chris is a person from now, confirmed. The car was full again, the way the car to Mom's had been full. I am not sure why the car keeps being full lately. I think it is that when I do not let the phone fill the gap, the gap fills with people instead, in the form of small thoughts of them, and the small thoughts of them are, it turns out, what I had been calling loneliness when I had been managing it with the phone.

The line moved. I was at the front by three-oh-six. Nina came out at three-eleven with her backpack over one shoulder and her coat unzipped despite it being thirty-eight degrees, and she got in and immediately began telling me a story about the substitute teacher in fourth period, who had pronounced a word wrong, and she narrated the entire arc — the wrong pronunciation, the kid who corrected her, the substitute's face, the recovery — for the first six minutes of the drive home, and I let her voice fill the car and did not say much, and at a stoplight I noticed I had spent twenty-one minutes in a parking lot without doing anything and was not, on inspection, the worse for it.

I had been told to come fifteen minutes early. The fifteen minutes had been, in the planning, a tax. They turned out to be a small unscheduled room. I am going to stop calling them the tax.

highlights.exact: So I am going to take it in, late. *Thanks, Beth.* Three days delayed, in a document she will never read, but on the record, in my own voice, where the voice that issues the verdicts can hear it. I was, on Thursday, in that kitchen, settled. Not solved. Not arrived. Settled, in the sense she meant it, which is the sense of someone who is, for a stretch of an afternoon, in the room they are in. That happened. She saw it. I am allowed to have it.
highlights.prefix: worse moods I cannot justify.
highlights.reasoning: Late acceptance of Beth's compliment in the privacy of the document

The Inner Critic.positiveIntent: Keep the day from going off the rails so I can be reliable for the people counting on me.
The Inner Critic.fearedOutcome: Letting something slip and watching someone notice — the small flicker of "oh, you didn't" that I can't un-see.
The Inner Critic.whatItProtects: A quieter part of me that goes blank when too many things are happening at once.
The Inner Critic.userNotes: Loudest on Sunday evenings reviewing the week ahead, and on Monday mornings when the inbox loads. Quieter on Friday afternoons, almost absent on long walks.

The Smoother.positiveIntent: Make sure no one I care about feels let down or under-prioritized.
The Smoother.fearedOutcome: Saying the wrong thing — or saying nothing — and losing the warmth of a relationship I depend on.
The Smoother.whatItProtects: A part of me that just wants to be liked, that has a hard time when someone is even mildly disappointed.
The Smoother.userNotes: Says yes before the rest of me has caught up. The resentment that arrives a day or two later is the tell.
